chapters.title: Motion
notes: Motion: Motion is the change in the position of an object with respect to its surroundings over time. An object is said to be in motion when its position changes relative to a reference point.
Distance: Distance is the total length of the actual path travelled by an object during motion. It is a scalar quantity and is always positive.
Displacement: Displacement is the shortest straight-line distance between the initial and final positions of an object. It is a vector quantity because it has both magnitude and direction.
Speed: Speed is the distance travelled by an object in unit time. It is a scalar quantity and does not depend on the direction of motion.
Velocity: Velocity is the displacement covered by an object in unit time. It is a vector quantity because both magnitude and direction are important.
Difference Between Speed and Velocity: Speed depends only on the distance travelled by an object. Velocity depends on both displacement and the direction of motion.
Uniform Motion: An object is said to be in uniform motion when it covers equal distances in equal intervals of time. Its speed remains constant throughout the motion.
Non-uniform Motion: An object is in non-uniform motion when it covers unequal distances in equal intervals of time. Its speed or direction changes during motion.
Acceleration: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity with time. It can occur when the speed, direction, or both change.

Positive, Negative and Zero Acceleration: Positive acceleration occurs when the velocity of an object increases with time. Negative acceleration (deceleration) occurs when velocity decreases, while zero acceleration means the velocity remains constant.

Distance-Time Graph: A distance-time graph shows how the distance travelled by an object changes with time. A straight line represents uniform motion, while a curved line represents non-uniform motion.
Velocity-Time Graph: A velocity-time graph shows how the velocity of an object changes with time. The area under the graph represents the distance travelled during that time.
Equations of Motion: The three equations of motion are — v = u + at | s = ut + ½at² | v² = u² + 2as, where u = initial velocity, v = final velocity, a = acceleration, t = time and s = displacement.

Uniform Circular Motion: Uniform circular motion is the motion of an object along a circular path with constant speed. Although the speed remains constant, the velocity changes continuously because the direction changes at every point.

Newton's First Law of Motion: Newton's First Law states that an object remains at rest or continues to move with uniform velocity in a straight line unless acted upon by an external unbalanced force. This law is also called the Law of Inertia.
Inertia: Inertia is the property of an object to resist any change in its state of rest or motion. Objects with greater mass have greater inertia.

Balanced and Unbalanced Forces: Balanced forces are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction, so they do not change the motion of an object. Unbalanced forces produce a net force that changes the speed or direction of motion.

Newton's Second Law of Motion: Newton's Second Law states that the rate of change of momentum is directly proportional to the applied force and takes place in the direction of the force. Formula: F = ma, where F = Force, m = Mass, a = Acceleration.
Momentum: Momentum is the product of the mass and velocity of an object. It is a vector quantity and its direction is the same as the direction of velocity. Formula: P = m × v.
SI Unit of Force: The SI unit of force is Newton (N). One Newton is the force required to produce an acceleration of 1 m/s² in a body of mass 1 kg.
Newton's Third Law of Motion: Newton's Third Law states that every action has an equal and opposite reaction. Action and reaction forces always act simultaneously on different objects.

Law of Conservation of Momentum: The total momentum of a system remains constant when no external force acts on it. During a collision, momentum is transferred from one object to another but the total momentum remains unchanged.

Everyday Applications: Seat belts protect passengers by reducing the effects of inertia during sudden stops. Rocket propulsion, gun recoil and walking are practical applications of Newton's Third Law of Motion.

flashcards.q: What is displacement?
flashcards.a: Displacement is the shortest straight-line distance between the initial and final positions of an object.

flashcards.q: What is acceleration?
flashcards.a: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity with time.

flashcards.q: State Newton's First Law of Motion.
flashcards.a: An object remains at rest or continues in uniform motion unless acted upon by an external unbalanced force.

flashcards.q: What is momentum?
flashcards.a: Momentum is the product of the mass and velocity of an object. P = m × v.

flashcards.q: State the Law of Conservation of Momentum.
flashcards.a: The total momentum of a system remains constant when no external force acts on it.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between distance and displacement?
flashcards.a: Distance is the total path length travelled (scalar), while displacement is the shortest straight-line distance between start and end points (vector).

flashcards.q: What is uniform circular motion?
flashcards.a: Motion of an object along a circular path with constant speed, where velocity changes direction at every point.

practice.q: Define distance and displacement. How do they differ?
practice.a:
Distance is the length of the actual path travelled by a moving object between two points.
Displacement is the shortest (minimum) distance between the starting and finishing points, taken along with direction.
Distance is always positive and can never decrease, while displacement can be zero even when distance travelled is not zero, and it has a direction associated with it.

practice.q: Define acceleration and state its SI unit.
practice.a:
Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity of a moving object, i.e.
Acceleration = (Change in velocity)/(Time taken).
If initial velocity is u and after time t it becomes v, then a = (v-u)/t.
Its SI unit is metre per second square (m/s²).

practice.q: State Newton's first law of motion.
practice.a:
Newton's first law of motion states that an object continues to remain at rest or in a state of uniform motion along a straight line unless an external unbalanced force acts on it.
It is also called the law of inertia.

practice.q: What is momentum? Give its formula and unit.
practice.a:
Momentum is the product of the mass and velocity of an object, P = m × v.
It is a vector quantity, having the same direction as the velocity.
Its SI unit is kg m/s.

practice.q: Define uniform circular motion.
practice.a:
When an object moves with a constant speed along a circular path, its motion is called uniform circular motion.
Although the speed remains constant, the direction of velocity keeps changing continuously, so the motion is accelerated.

practice.q: State and explain Newton's third law of motion with an example.
practice.a:
Newton's third law states that every action force has an equal and opposite reaction force which acts simultaneously.
Forces always occur in pairs;
when one object exerts a force on a second object, the second object exerts an equal and opposite force on the first.
For example, when a bullet is fired from a gun, the gun exerts a forward force (action) on the bullet, and the bullet exerts an equal backward force (reaction) on the gun, causing the gun to recoil.

practice.q: Derive the first equation of motion (v = u + at) using the velocity-time graph method.
practice.a:
Consider a velocity-time graph where an object starts with initial velocity u (point D) and after time t reaches final velocity v (point B), moving with uniform acceleration a.
From the graph, acceleration a = (change in velocity)/time = (OC - OD)/t = CD/t, so CD = at.
Since v = CD + OD (as AB = CD and AE = OD), we get v = at + u, i.e. v = u + at.
This is the first equation of motion, giving the relation between velocity and time.

practice.q: State the law of conservation of momentum and give one example from everyday life.
practice.a:
The law of conservation of momentum states that when no external force acts on two interacting objects, their total momentum before the interaction is equal to their total momentum after the interaction;
the total momentum remains constant.
Example: when a bullet is fired from a gun, before firing the total momentum of the gun-bullet system is zero (both at rest).
After firing, the forward momentum of the bullet is balanced by an equal and opposite backward momentum of the recoiling gun, so the total momentum remains zero.

practice.q: Derive the second equation of motion s = ut + ½at² using the graphical method.
practice.a:
Consider the velocity-time graph of an object moving with uniform acceleration a, starting with initial velocity u at time 0, reaching velocity v at time t.
The distance covered s is given by the area under the velocity-time graph, which is the area of quadrangle DOEB.
This area = area of rectangle DOEA + area of triangle DAB.
Area of rectangle DOEA = OE × AE = t × u = ut.
Area of triangle DAB = ½ × AB × DA = ½ × at × t = ½at² (since AB = at from the first equation, and DA = t).
Therefore, s = ut + ½at².
This is Newton's second equation of motion, relating displacement and time.

practice.q: Solve: An object of mass 16 kg is moving with an acceleration of 3 m/s². Calculate the applied force. If the same force is applied on an object of mass 24 kg, how much will be the acceleration?
practice.a:
Given: m₁ = 16 kg, a₁ = 3 m/s², m₂ = 24 kg.
Force applied, F = m₁ × a₁ = 16 × 3 = 48 N.
When the same force F = 48 N acts on mass m₂ = 24 kg: a₂ = F/m₂ = 48/24 = 2 m/s².
Thus, the applied force is 48 N, and the acceleration produced in the 24 kg object is 2 m/s².

chapters.title: Work and Energy
notes: Work: Work is said to be done when a force applied on an object causes it to move in the direction of the force. If there is no displacement, no work is done even if a force is applied.
Conditions for Work to be Done: Work is done only when a force acts on an object and the object moves due to that force. If either force or displacement is absent, the work done is zero.
Formula for Work: Work = Force × Displacement. W = F × s, where W = Work, F = Force, s = Displacement. The SI unit of work is Joule (J).
Positive Work: Positive work is done when the force and displacement act in the same direction. The work done increases as the force or displacement increases.
Negative Work: Negative work is done when the force acts opposite to the direction of displacement. Friction is a common example because it opposes motion.

Zero Work: Zero work is done when there is no displacement or when the force acts perpendicular to the direction of motion. Carrying a school bag while walking on a level road is an example of zero work done by the upward force.
Energy: Energy is the capacity of an object to do work. Energy can neither be created nor destroyed but can only be converted from one form to another.
Forms of Energy: Energy exists in different forms such as mechanical, heat, light, sound, electrical and chemical energy. These forms can be transformed from one into another.
Mechanical Energy: Mechanical energy is the sum of kinetic energy and potential energy possessed by an object. It depends on the object's motion and position.
Kinetic Energy: Kinetic energy is the energy possessed by an object because of its motion. Formula: KE = ½mv², where m = Mass and v = Velocity.

Potential Energy: Potential energy is the energy possessed by an object due to its position or height above the ground. Formula: PE = mgh, where m = Mass, g = acceleration due to gravity and h = Height.
Transformation of Energy: Energy can change from one form to another without any loss of total energy. For example, a falling object converts potential energy into kinetic energy.
Law of Conservation of Energy: Energy can neither be created nor destroyed. The total amount of energy always remains constant during any physical process.
Power: Power is the rate at which work is done or energy is transferred. Formula: P = W/t, where P = Power, W = Work, t = Time. The SI unit of power is Watt (W).
SI Unit of Power: One watt is the power of a device that does one joule of work in one second.
Commercial Unit of Electrical Energy: Electrical energy used in homes is measured in kilowatt-hour (kWh). One kilowatt-hour = 3.6 × 10⁶ J.
Simple Machines: Simple machines help us perform work with less effort by changing the magnitude or direction of the applied force. They make work easier but do not reduce the total work done.
Lever: A lever is a rigid bar that rotates about a fixed point called the fulcrum. Depending on the positions of the fulcrum, load and effort, levers are classified into three types.
Mechanical Advantage: Mechanical advantage is the ratio of load lifted by a machine to the effort applied. Formula: MA = Load ÷ Effort. A higher MA means less effort is needed.

Efficiency of a Machine: Efficiency is the ratio of useful work obtained from a machine to the work supplied to it. No machine is 100% efficient because some energy is always lost due to friction. Formula: Efficiency = (Useful Work Output ÷ Work Input) × 100%.

Everyday Applications: Hydroelectric power plants convert the potential energy of stored water into electrical energy. Fans, mixers and electric motors convert electrical energy into mechanical energy.

flashcards.q: What is work?
flashcards.a: Work is done when a force causes an object to move in the direction of the applied force. W = F × s.

flashcards.q: What is kinetic energy?
flashcards.a: Kinetic energy is the energy possessed by an object due to its motion. KE = ½mv².

flashcards.q: State the Law of Conservation of Energy.
flashcards.a: Energy can neither be created nor destroyed but can only be converted from one form to another.

flashcards.q: What is power?
flashcards.a: Power is the rate at which work is done or energy is transferred. P = W/t.

flashcards.q: What is mechanical advantage?
flashcards.a: Mechanical advantage is the ratio of load lifted by a machine to the effort applied. MA = Load ÷ Effort.

flashcards.q: What is the commercial unit of electrical energy?
flashcards.a: The commercial unit of electrical energy is kilowatt-hour (kWh). 1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J.

flashcards.q: Give one example of energy transformation.
flashcards.a: A falling object converts potential energy (PE) into kinetic energy (KE).

practice.q: Define work done by a force and give its SI unit.
practice.a:
Work is said to be done when a force applied on an object causes displacement of the object.
Work = Force × displacement in the direction of the force (W = F × s).
The SI unit of work is the joule (J);
1 joule of work is done when a force of 1 newton displaces an object through 1 metre in the direction of the force.

practice.q: Define kinetic energy and write its formula.
practice.a:
The energy possessed by an object due to its motion is called kinetic energy.
It equals the work done to bring the object from rest to its current velocity.
Kinetic energy, K.E. = ½mv², where m is the mass and v is the velocity of the object.

practice.q: State the law of conservation of energy.
practice.a:
The law of conservation of energy states that energy can neither be created nor destroyed;
it can only be converted from one form to another.
Thus, the total amount of energy in the universe remains constant.

practice.q: Define power and state its SI unit.
practice.a:
Power is the rate at which work is done, Power = Work done/Time taken (P = W/t).
The SI unit of power is the watt (W);
1 watt = 1 joule/1 second.
A larger commercial unit is the kilowatt-hour, used to measure electrical energy consumption.

practice.q: When is the work done by a force said to be zero?
practice.a: Work done by a force is zero when the applied force does not cause any displacement of the object, or when the force and displacement are perpendicular to each other (angle θ = 90°), since W = Fs cos θ and cos 90° = 0.

practice.q: Derive the formula for potential energy of an object of mass m raised to a height h.
practice.a:
To raise an object of mass m to a height h against gravity, a force equal to mg must be applied against the gravitational force.
The work done in raising the object, W = Force × displacement = mg × h = mgh.
Since this work gets stored in the object as potential energy due to its position, the potential energy P.E. = mgh.

practice.q: Explain, with an example, how energy gets transformed from one form to another.
practice.a:
Energy can change from one form into another while the total energy remains constant, according to the law of conservation of energy.
For example, in an electric fan, electrical energy is converted into mechanical (kinetic) energy of the rotating blades and some heat energy.
Similarly, when firecrackers explode, the chemical energy stored in them is converted into light, sound and heat energy.
Such transformation of energy takes place in most machines and natural processes.

practice.q: Prove that the total energy of a freely falling object remains constant throughout its fall.
practice.a:
Consider an object of mass m falling freely from height h.
At the top (point A), it is at rest so K.E. = 0 and P.E. = mgh;
total energy = mgh.
At an intermediate point B, having fallen a distance x, using v² = u² + 2as, velocity vB² = 2gx, so K.E. = mgx, and P.E. = mg(h-x);
total energy = mgx + mgh - mgx = mgh.
At the ground (point C), velocity vC² = 2gh, so K.E. = mgh and P.E. = 0 (height = 0);
total energy = mgh.
Thus, at all points A, B and C, the total energy (P.E. + K.E.) remains equal to mgh, proving that total mechanical energy is conserved during free fall.

practice.q: Derive the expression for kinetic energy of an object of mass m moving with velocity v.
practice.a:
Consider a stationary object of mass m (initial velocity u = 0) which starts moving due to an applied force F, producing acceleration a.
After time t, its velocity becomes v, and its displacement is s.
Work done on the object, W = F × s.
By Newton's second law, F = ma.
By the second equation of motion, since u = 0, s = ½at².
So W = ma × ½at² = ½m(at)².
By the first equation of motion, since u = 0, v = at, so v² = (at)².
Therefore W = ½mv².
Since the kinetic energy gained equals the work done on the object, K.E. = ½mv².

practice.q: Solve: Swaralee takes 20 s to carry a bag weighing 20 kg to a height of 5 m. How much power has she used? (g = 9.8 m/s²)
practice.a:
Given: m = 20 kg, h = 5 m, t = 20 s, g = 9.8 m/s².
Force required, F = mg = 20 × 9.8 = 196 N.
Work done, W = F × s = 196 × 5 = 980 J.
Power, P = W/t = 980/20 = 49 W.
Thus, Swaralee used 49 watts of power to carry the bag to the height of 5 m in 20 seconds.

chapters.title: Current Electricity
notes: Electric Charge: Electric charge is a fundamental property of matter responsible for electrical attraction and repulsion. There are two types: positive and negative.
Static Electricity: Static electricity is produced when electric charges accumulate on the surface of an object. It is commonly produced by rubbing two different materials together.

Electric Current: Electric current is the continuous flow of electric charges through a conductor. It flows only when a complete conducting path is available. Formula: I = Q/t, where I = Current, Q = Charge, t = Time. SI unit: Ampere (A).
Electric Circuit: An electric circuit is a closed path through which electric current flows from the source to the electrical components. If the circuit is broken, the current stops flowing.
Components of an Electric Circuit: A simple circuit consists of a cell, connecting wires, a switch and an electrical device such as a bulb. Each component performs a specific function.
Electric Cell: An electric cell converts chemical energy into electrical energy. It has two terminals: positive and negative.
Potential Difference: Potential difference is the work done to move a unit positive charge between two points. SI unit: Volt (V). It is measured using a voltmeter.
Resistance: Resistance is the property of a conductor that opposes the flow of electric current. It depends on the material, length, thickness and temperature of the conductor.
Conductors and Insulators: Conductors allow electric current to pass through them easily (e.g. copper, aluminium). Insulators do not allow current to pass (e.g. rubber, plastic, glass).

Ohm's Law: Current through a conductor is directly proportional to the potential difference across it at constant temperature. Formula: V = IR, where V = Potential Difference, I = Current, R = Resistance.
Resistance in Series: When resistors are connected one after another, total resistance = R₁ + R₂ + R₃ + ...
Resistance in Parallel: When resistors are connected across the same two points, 1/R = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ + 1/R₃ + ... The equivalent resistance is always less than the smallest individual resistance.
Heating Effect of Electric Current: When current flows through a conductor, electrical energy converts into heat energy. This effect is used in electric irons, heaters and kettles.
Electric Fuse: An electric fuse is a safety device that protects appliances from excessive current by melting and breaking the circuit.
Miniature Circuit Breaker (MCB): An MCB is an automatic safety device that disconnects the circuit during overload or short circuit. Unlike a fuse, it can be reset and reused.
Short Circuit: A short circuit occurs when the live wire comes into direct contact with the neutral wire, causing a sudden increase in current and risk of fire.
Electromagnet: An electromagnet is a temporary magnet produced by passing electric current through a coil wound around a soft iron core. It loses magnetism when the current is switched off.
Magnetic Effect of Electric Current: A current-carrying conductor produces a magnetic field around it. This is used in electric bells, cranes and loudspeakers.
Fleming's Right-Hand Thumb Rule: When the thumb points in the direction of current, the curled fingers indicate the direction of the magnetic field around the conductor.
Safety While Using Electricity: Handle appliances with dry hands. Avoid damaged wires and overloaded sockets to prevent electric shock.

flashcards.q: What is electric current?
flashcards.a: Electric current is the continuous flow of electric charges through a conductor. I = Q/t.

flashcards.q: State Ohm's Law.
flashcards.a: Current is directly proportional to potential difference at constant temperature. V = IR.

flashcards.q: What is an electromagnet?
flashcards.a: A temporary magnet produced by passing electric current through a coil wound around a soft iron core.

flashcards.q: What is the function of an electric fuse?
flashcards.a: An electric fuse protects circuits by melting and breaking the circuit when excessive current flows.

flashcards.q: What is the magnetic effect of electric current?
flashcards.a: A current-carrying conductor produces a magnetic field around it.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between series and parallel resistance?
flashcards.a: In series: R = R₁ + R₂ + R₃. In parallel: 1/R = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ + 1/R₃.

flashcards.q: What is potential difference?
flashcards.a: Work done to move a unit positive charge between two points. Measured in Volts (V).

practice.q: Define electric current and state its SI unit.
practice.a:
Electric current is the rate of flow of charge through a conductor.
If charge Q flows through a conductor in time t, current I = Q/t.
The SI unit of current is the ampere (A);
one ampere current flows when one coulomb of charge passes through a conductor in one second.

practice.q: State Ohm's law.
practice.a:
Ohm's law states that if the physical state of a conductor (length, area of cross-section, temperature and material) remains constant, the current (I) flowing through it is directly proportional to the potential difference (V) applied between its two ends.
Thus V = IR, where R is the resistance of the conductor.

practice.q: Define resistance and state its unit.
practice.a:
Resistance is the property of a conductor that opposes the flow of current through it, caused by collisions of moving electrons with atoms and ions in the conductor.
Its SI unit is the ohm (Ω);
a conductor has a resistance of one ohm if a current of one ampere flows through it when a potential difference of one volt is applied across its ends.

practice.q: Distinguish between conductors and insulators.
practice.a:
Conductors are substances having very low resistance, through which current flows easily, e.g. copper, aluminium.
Insulators (or bad conductors) are substances having extremely high resistance, through which current cannot flow, e.g. rubber, glass, plastic.

practice.q: What is the function of a fuse wire in a domestic electric circuit?
practice.a:
A fuse wire is connected in series in a domestic circuit to protect appliances.
It is made of a material with a low melting point.
If the current increases excessively due to a fault, the fuse wire heats up and melts, breaking the circuit and stopping the flow of current, thereby protecting the appliances from damage.

practice.q: Derive the formula for effective resistance when resistors are connected in series.
practice.a:
When resistors R1, R2 and R3 are connected in series, the same current I flows through each.
If V1, V2, V3 are the potential differences across them, the total potential difference V = V1 + V2 + V3.
By Ohm's law, V1 = IR1, V2 = IR2, V3 = IR3, and if RS is the effective (series) resistance, V = IRS.
Substituting, IRS = IR1 + IR2 + IR3, so RS = R1 + R2 + R3.
In general, for n resistors in series, RS = R1 + R2 + ... + Rn.

practice.q: Explain resistivity and state the factors on which the resistance of a conductor depends.
practice.a:
The resistance R of a conductor at a given temperature depends directly on its length L and inversely on its area of cross-section A: R ∝ L/A, so R = ρL/A, where ρ (rho) is the constant of proportionality called resistivity.
Resistivity is a specific property of the material of the conductor (different materials have different resistivity) and does not depend on the conductor's dimensions.
The SI unit of resistivity is ohm-metre (Ω m).

practice.q: Explain, with reasoning, why domestic electrical appliances are connected in parallel rather than in series.
practice.a:
In a domestic circuit, appliances are connected in parallel so that (1) the potential difference across each appliance remains the same (equal to the supply voltage, about 220 V), allowing each appliance to work at its rated voltage;
(2) if one appliance stops working or is switched off, the circuit for other appliances is not broken, since each has an independent path;
and (3) each appliance can be operated independently with its own switch.
If appliances were connected in series, all would need the same current, the voltage would be divided amongst them, and if one failed the entire circuit would break.

practice.q: Derive the formula for effective resistance when resistors are connected in parallel.
practice.a:
When resistors R1, R2 and R3 are connected in parallel between points C and D, the potential difference V across each is the same, but the currents through them, I1, I2, I3, may differ.
The total current I = I1 + I2 + I3.
By Ohm's law, I1 = V/R1, I2 = V/R2, I3 = V/R3, and I = V/RP, where RP is the effective (parallel) resistance.
Substituting, V/RP = V/R1 + V/R2 + V/R3, so 1/RP = 1/R1 + 1/R2 + 1/R3.
In general, for n resistors in parallel, 1/RP = 1/R1 + 1/R2 + ... + 1/Rn.
The effective resistance in parallel is always smaller than the smallest individual resistance.

practice.q: Solve: Three resistors having resistances of 5 Ω, 10 Ω and 30 Ω are connected in parallel, and a potential difference of 12 V is applied across them. Find the current through each resistor, the total current, and the effective resistance of the circuit.
practice.a:
Given: R1 = 5 Ω, R2 = 10 Ω, R3 = 30 Ω, V = 12 V.
Current through R1: I1 = V/R1 = 12/5 = 2.4 A Current through R2: I2 = V/R2 = 12/10 = 1.2 A Current through R3: I3 = V/R3 = 12/30 = 0.4 A Total current, I = I1 + I2 + I3 = 2.4 + 1.2 + 0.4 = 4.0 A Effective resistance: 1/RP = 1/5 + 1/10 + 1/30 = 6/30 + 3/30 + 1/30 = 10/30 = 1/3, so RP = 3 Ω. Thus, the currents are 2.4 A, 1.2 A and 0.4 A, the total current is 4 A, and the effective resistance is 3 Ω.

chapters.title: Measurement of Matter
notes: Matter: Matter is anything that has mass and occupies space. Everything around us is made up of matter.
Physical Classification of Matter: Matter is classified into solids, liquids and gases based on the arrangement of its particles.
Solid State: Solids have a definite shape and volume because particles are closely packed. Particles can only vibrate about their fixed positions.
Liquid State: Liquids have a definite volume but no definite shape. Their particles can move freely and they take the shape of the container.
Gaseous State: Gases have neither definite shape nor volume because particles are far apart. They spread out and fill the entire container.
Intermolecular Force: The force of attraction between the particles of matter. It is strongest in solids, weaker in liquids and weakest in gases.
Chemical Classification of Matter: Based on chemical composition, matter is classified into elements, compounds and mixtures.
Element: A pure substance made up of only one type of atom. It cannot be broken down by ordinary chemical methods.
Compound: A pure substance formed when two or more elements combine chemically in a fixed proportion. Its properties differ from those of its constituent elements.
Mixture: Formed when two or more substances are mixed physically without a chemical reaction. Components can be separated by physical methods.
Homogeneous Mixture: Has uniform composition throughout. Salt solution and sugar solution are examples.
Heterogeneous Mixture: Has non-uniform composition; components can often be seen separately. Sand mixed with water is an example.
Organic Compounds: Carbon-containing compounds found in living organisms. Examples include glucose, starch and proteins.
Inorganic Compounds: Generally do not contain carbon as their main element. Water, common salt and washing soda are examples.
Mass: The amount of matter present in an object. It remains the same regardless of location.
Weight: The force with which the Earth attracts an object due to gravity. Weight changes with changes in gravitational force.
Volume: The amount of space occupied by an object. Measured in cubic units such as m³ or cm³.
Density: Mass of a substance per unit volume. Formula: ρ = m/V. Substances with higher density have more mass in the same volume.
Measuring Volume of Irregular Solids: Measured using the water displacement method. The increase in water level equals the volume of the solid.
SI Units: Scientists use the International System of Units. Common SI units include metre (m), kilogram (kg), second (s) and cubic metre (m³).
Importance of Accurate Measurement: Accurate measurement ensures reliable experimental results and helps reduce errors during practical work.

flashcards.q: What is matter?
flashcards.a: Matter is anything that has mass and occupies space.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between an element and a compound?
flashcards.a: An element contains only one type of atom. A compound is formed by the chemical combination of two or more elements in a fixed proportion.

flashcards.q: What is density?
flashcards.a: Density is the mass of a substance per unit volume. ρ = m/V.

flashcards.q: What is a homogeneous mixture?
flashcards.a: A mixture that has uniform composition throughout, such as salt solution.

flashcards.q: How is the volume of an irregular solid measured?
flashcards.a: Using the water displacement method — the increase in water level equals the volume of the solid.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between mass and weight?
flashcards.a: Mass is the amount of matter in an object and does not change with location. Weight is the gravitational force acting on an object and changes with location.

flashcards.q: Name three states of matter and give one property of each.
flashcards.a: Solid — definite shape and volume. Liquid — definite volume but no definite shape. Gas — neither definite shape nor volume.

practice.q: State the law of conservation of matter.
practice.a:
The law of conservation of matter, given by Antoine Lavoisier in 1785, states that there is no rise or drop in the mass of matter during a chemical reaction;
the total mass of the reactants is equal to the total mass of the products formed.

practice.q: State the law of constant proportion.
practice.a:
The law of constant proportion, given by J.
L.
Proust in 1794, states that the proportion by mass of the constituent elements in different samples of a compound is always fixed, irrespective of the source or method of preparation of the compound.
For example, the ratio of hydrogen to oxygen by mass in water obtained from any source is always 1:8.

practice.q: What is meant by 'mole'? Give its SI unit.
practice.a:
A mole is that quantity of a substance whose mass in grams is numerically equal to the molecular mass of that substance in Daltons (u).
Its SI unit is 'mol'. For example, since the molecular mass of oxygen is 32 u, one mole of oxygen has a mass of 32 g.

practice.q: Define valency of an element.
practice.a:
Valency is the capacity of an element to combine with other elements, indicated by a specific number.
It is the number of electrons that an atom of an element gives away or takes up (or shares) while forming a chemical bond with another atom.

practice.q: What are radicals? Give one example each of a basic and an acidic radical.
practice.a:
Radicals are the charged constituents (cations and anions) of an ionic compound that take part independently in chemical reactions.
Cations (positively charged) are called basic radicals, e.g.
Na⁺;
anions (negatively charged) are called acidic radicals, e.g.
Cl⁻.

practice.q: Explain Avogadro's number with an example.
practice.a:
The number of molecules present in one mole of any substance is a constant, called Avogadro's number, denoted NA, with a value of 6.022 × 10²³.
Just as a dozen means 12 items, a mole means 6.022 × 10²³ particles.
For example, one mole of water (18 g of water) contains 6.022 × 10²³ molecules of water;
similarly, 32 g of oxygen (one mole) also contains 6.022 × 10²³ molecules, even though the mass differs from that of water.

practice.q: Explain with an example how the molecular mass of a compound is calculated.
practice.a:
Molecular mass is the sum of the atomic masses of all the atoms present in one molecule of a substance, expressed in Dalton (u).
For example, for water (H2O): there are 2 hydrogen atoms (atomic mass 1 each) and 1 oxygen atom (atomic mass 16).
Molecular mass of H2O = (2 × 1) + (1 × 16) = 2 + 16 = 18 u.

practice.q: Explain the steps for writing the chemical formula of a compound using the cross-multiplication method, with sodium sulphate as an example.
practice.a:
Step 1: Write the symbols of the basic radical (cation) on the left and acidic radical (anion) on the right: Na, SO4.
Step 2: Write the valency of each radical below it: Na has valency 1, SO4 has valency 2.
Step 3: Cross-multiply the valencies, i.e. the valency of one radical becomes the subscript of the other.
Step 4: Write the resulting formula: Na2SO4 (sodium sulphate).

practice.q: Explain how the law of constant proportion was verified experimentally using copper oxide obtained from two different sources.
practice.a:
Two samples of copper oxide (CuO) were prepared by different methods: one by decomposition of copper carbonate and another by decomposition of copper nitrate.
From each sample, 8 g of copper oxide was treated with hydrogen gas;
both samples gave 6.4 g of copper and 1.8 g of water.
Since water (H2O) has hydrogen and oxygen in the ratio 1:8 by mass, 1.8 g water contains (8×1.8)/9 = 1.6 g of oxygen.
This oxygen came from the 8 g copper oxide, so the 8 g copper oxide contained 6.4 g copper and 1.6 g oxygen, giving a mass ratio of Cu:O = 6.4:1.6 = 4:1.
This matches the ratio calculated from the molecular formula CuO using atomic masses (Cu = 63.5, O = 16), which gives 63.5:16 ≈ 4:1.
Since both samples, obtained differently, gave the same proportion, the law of constant proportion is verified.

practice.q: Deduce the number of molecules present in 66 g of carbon dioxide (CO2). (Molecular mass of CO2 = 44, Avogadro's number = 6.022 × 10²³)
practice.a:
Given: Mass of CO2 = 66 g, Molecular mass of CO2 = 44 u.
Number of moles, n = Mass in grams / Molecular mass = 66/44 = 1.5 mol.
Since 1 mole of any substance contains 6.022 × 10²³ molecules, Number of molecules in 1.5 mol CO2 = 1.5 × 6.022 × 10²³ = 9.033 × 10²³ molecules.
Thus, 66 g of CO2 contains 9.033 × 10²³ molecules.

chapters.title: Acids, Bases and Salts
notes: Acids: Acids are substances that produce hydrogen ions (H⁺) when dissolved in water. They generally have a sour taste and turn blue litmus paper red.
Properties of Acids: Acids react with metals to produce hydrogen gas and react with bases to form salts and water. Strong acids ionize completely; weak acids ionize partially.
Common Acids: Hydrochloric acid (HCl), sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄) and nitric acid (HNO₃) are mineral acids. Citric acid in lemons and acetic acid in vinegar are natural acids.
Bases: Bases produce hydroxide ions (OH⁻) when dissolved in water. They usually have a bitter taste and feel soapy to touch.
Properties of Bases: Bases turn red litmus paper blue and neutralize acids to form salt and water. Strong bases are highly reactive.
Alkalis: Alkalis are bases that dissolve in water and produce hydroxide ions. Sodium hydroxide and potassium hydroxide are common examples.
Indicators: Substances that change colour in the presence of acids and bases to help identify whether a solution is acidic or basic.
Litmus Indicator: A natural indicator obtained from lichens. Blue litmus turns red in acids; red litmus turns blue in bases.
Phenolphthalein: Remains colourless in acidic solutions and turns pink in basic solutions.
Methyl Orange: Turns red in acidic solutions and yellow in basic solutions.
Universal Indicator: Shows different colours depending on the strength of an acid or base. Used to estimate the pH value.
pH Scale: Measures how acidic or basic a solution is. Ranges from 0 to 14. pH 7 = neutral, below 7 = acidic, above 7 = basic.
Neutralization Reaction: Occurs when an acid reacts with a base to form salt and water. General equation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water.
Salts: Ionic compounds formed during a neutralization reaction. Different acid-base combinations produce different salts.
Common Salts: Common salt (NaCl), baking soda and washing soda are widely used in daily life.
Uses of Acids: Used in manufacturing fertilizers, medicines, dyes and batteries, and in food preservation and industrial processes.
Uses of Bases: Used in making soaps, detergents, paper and medicines. Also used to neutralize acidic substances.
Everyday Applications: Antacids contain weak bases that neutralize excess stomach acid. Farmers add lime to acidic soil to improve fertility.

flashcards.q: What is an acid?
flashcards.a: An acid is a substance that produces hydrogen ions (H⁺) when dissolved in water.

flashcards.q: What is a base?
flashcards.a: A base is a substance that produces hydroxide ions (OH⁻) when dissolved in water.

flashcards.q: What is a neutralization reaction?
flashcards.a: A reaction in which an acid reacts with a base to form salt and water.

flashcards.q: What is the pH scale?
flashcards.a: The pH scale measures the acidity or basicity of a solution on a scale from 0 to 14. pH 7 = neutral.

flashcards.q: What are indicators?
flashcards.a: Substances that change colour to show whether a solution is acidic or basic.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between strong and weak acids?
flashcards.a: Strong acids ionize completely in water; weak acids ionize only partially.

flashcards.q: What are alkalis?
flashcards.a: Alkalis are bases that dissolve in water and produce hydroxide ions. Examples: NaOH, KOH.

practice.q: Define an acid and a base according to the Arrhenius theory.
practice.a:
According to the Arrhenius theory, an acid is a substance which on dissolving in water gives rise to H⁺ ion as the only cation, e.g.
HCl.
A base is a substance which on dissolving in water gives rise to OH⁻ ion as the only anion, e.g.
NaOH.

practice.q: What is meant by the pH of a solution?
practice.a:
pH (power of hydrogen) is a scale, ranging from 0 to 14, used to express the concentration of H⁺ ions in a solution.
Pure water has a pH of 7, indicating a neutral solution.
A pH less than 7 indicates an acidic solution, and a pH greater than 7 indicates a basic solution.

practice.q: What is meant by neutralization reaction? Give one example.
practice.a:
A neutralization reaction is a reaction between an acid and a base to form a salt and water.
For example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H2O, in which hydrochloric acid reacts with sodium hydroxide to form sodium chloride (salt) and water.

practice.q: What is water of crystallization? Give one example.
practice.a:
Water of crystallization is the fixed number of water molecules chemically bound within the crystal structure of certain salts, present in a definite proportion in the chemical formula.
For example, blue vitriol (crystalline copper sulphate) is represented as CuSO4.5H2O, containing 5 molecules of water of crystallization.

practice.q: What is electrolysis?
practice.a:
Electrolysis is the decomposition of a solute in a solution (or a molten compound) into its constituent ions/elements by passing an electric current through it.
It involves two processes occurring simultaneously: a cathode reaction and an anode reaction.

practice.q: Distinguish between strong and weak acids with examples.
practice.a:
A strong acid dissociates almost completely in its aqueous solution, giving a large concentration of H⁺ ions and the corresponding acidic radical, e.g.
HCl, HNO3, H2SO4.
A weak acid dissociates only partially in its aqueous solution;
it contains a small proportion of H⁺ ions along with a large proportion of undissociated acid molecules, e.g.
CH3COOH (acetic acid), carbonic acid (H2CO3).

practice.q: Explain the classification of salts into acidic, basic and neutral types based on the nature of the acid and base used to form them.
practice.a:
A neutral salt is formed by the neutralization of a strong acid with a strong base;
its aqueous solution has a pH equal to 7, e.g.
NaCl (from HCl and NaOH).
An acidic salt is formed by the neutralization of a strong acid with a weak base;
its aqueous solution has a pH less than 7, e.g. ammonium chloride (from HCl and NH4OH).
A basic salt is formed by the neutralization of a weak acid with a strong base;
its aqueous solution has a pH greater than 7, e.g. sodium acetate (from acetic acid and NaOH).

practice.q: Explain electrolysis of water with the help of the electrode reactions.
practice.a:
When electric current is passed through slightly acidified or salted water using two electrodes, gas bubbles form at both electrodes: hydrogen gas is released at the cathode and oxygen gas at the anode, in a volume ratio of 2:1 respectively.
The electrode reactions are: Cathode reaction: 2H2O(l) + 2e⁻ → H2(g) + 2OH⁻(aq);
Anode reaction: 2H2O(l) → O2(g) + 4H⁺(aq) + 4e⁻. This shows that pure water itself dissociates to a very small extent, but the presence of a small amount of salt or acid increases its conductivity enough to allow visible electrolysis of water into its constituent elements, hydrogen and oxygen.

practice.q: Explain, with a chemical equation, what happens when dilute NaOH solution is added drop by drop to dilute HCl solution, and why the pH of the solution changes during this process.
practice.a:
Both HCl and NaOH dissociate almost completely in their aqueous solutions: HCl → H⁺ + Cl⁻ and NaOH → Na⁺ + OH⁻. When NaOH solution is added drop by drop to the HCl solution, the added OH⁻ ions combine with the H⁺ ions present in excess to form un-ionized water molecules: H⁺ + OH⁻ → H2O.
Since water dissociates only to a very small extent, this removes H⁺ ions from solution, so the concentration of H⁺ ions keeps decreasing and the pH of the solution keeps increasing (moving from acidic towards neutral, pH 7).
The overall reaction is HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H2O.
When just enough NaOH has been added to react completely with all the HCl, the resulting solution contains only Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions (i.e. the neutral salt NaCl) and water;
this stage, where the pH becomes 7, is called the neutralization point.
This is why the process is called a neutralization reaction.

practice.q: Describe, with the help of a labelled activity, how you would verify experimentally whether a given salt solution is acidic, basic or neutral, and classify sodium chloride, ammonium chloride and sodium bicarbonate accordingly.
practice.a:
Apparatus/Materials: Beakers, sodium chloride, ammonium chloride, sodium bicarbonate, distilled water, pH paper (or universal indicator solution), glass rod.
Procedure: Prepare separate 10 mL aqueous solutions of 1 g each of sodium chloride, ammonium chloride and sodium bicarbonate in water.
Using a glass rod, place a drop of each solution on a separate strip of pH paper and compare the colour developed with the standard pH colour chart to note the pH value of each.
Observation and Classification: Sodium chloride (NaCl) is formed from a strong acid (HCl) and a strong base (NaOH);
its solution shows a pH of about 7, so it is a neutral salt.
Ammonium chloride (NH4Cl) is formed from a strong acid (HCl) and a weak base (NH4OH);
its solution shows a pH less than 7, so it is an acidic salt.
Sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3) is formed from a weak acid (H2CO3) and a strong base (NaOH);
its solution shows a pH greater than 7, so it is a basic salt.
Conclusion: The nature (acidic/basic/neutral) of a salt's aqueous solution depends on the relative strengths of the acid and base from which the salt is formed.

chapters.title: Classification of Plants
notes: Classification of Plants: Plants are classified into different groups based on structure, method of reproduction and presence of vascular tissues. Classification makes scientific study systematic.
Cryptogams: Plants that reproduce by spores and do not produce flowers or seeds. They are generally simple in structure and grow in moist places.
Phanerogams: Plants that produce seeds and have well-developed reproductive organs. They are more advanced than cryptogams.
Thallophyta: The simplest plants whose body is not differentiated into roots, stems and leaves. Most are found in water and reproduce through spores.
Algae: Green, chlorophyll-containing organisms that prepare food by photosynthesis. Mainly aquatic and release oxygen into the environment.
Bryophyta: Small, non-vascular plants that grow in moist and shady places. Known as 'amphibians of the plant kingdom' because they require water for reproduction.
Pteridophyta: The first vascular plants with true roots, stems and leaves. Reproduce through spores instead of seeds.
Gymnosperms: Seed-producing plants whose seeds are not enclosed within fruits. Most are evergreen trees with needle-like leaves.
Angiosperms: Flowering plants whose seeds are enclosed within fruits. The largest and most diverse group of plants on Earth.
Monocotyledons: Flowering plants whose seeds contain one cotyledon. They have parallel leaf venation and fibrous root systems.
Dicotyledons: Flowering plants whose seeds contain two cotyledons. They have reticulate leaf venation and tap root systems.
Vascular Tissue: Consists of xylem and phloem that transport water, minerals and food throughout the plant.
Xylem: Transports water and dissolved minerals from roots to different parts of the plant. Also provides mechanical support.
Phloem: Transports food prepared in leaves to all parts of the plant.
Spore Formation: Tiny reproductive structures produced by cryptogams that germinate to form new plants under favourable conditions.
Seed Formation: Seeds are formed after fertilization and contain the embryo and stored food for new plant growth.
Importance of Plant Classification: Helps identify plants, understand evolutionary relationships and makes scientific study organized.
Economic Importance of Plants: Plants provide food, oxygen, medicines, timber, fibres and many useful products.
Differences — Cryptogams vs Phanerogams: Cryptogams reproduce by spores without flowers or seeds. Phanerogams reproduce by seeds with well-developed reproductive structures.

flashcards.q: What are cryptogams?
flashcards.a: Cryptogams are plants that reproduce by spores and do not produce flowers or seeds.

flashcards.q: What are angiosperms?
flashcards.a: Angiosperms are flowering plants whose seeds are enclosed within fruits.

flashcards.q: What is the function of xylem?
flashcards.a: Xylem transports water and minerals from the roots to different parts of the plant.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between monocotyledons and dicotyledons?
flashcards.a: Monocotyledons have one cotyledon and parallel venation. Dicotyledons have two cotyledons and reticulate venation.

flashcards.q: Why is plant classification important?
flashcards.a: It helps identify plants, understand their characteristics and makes scientific study easier.

flashcards.q: Why are bryophytes called amphibians of the plant kingdom?
flashcards.a: Because they need water for reproduction, just like amphibians need water for part of their life cycle.

flashcards.q: What are gymnosperms?
flashcards.a: Seed-producing plants whose seeds are not enclosed within fruits. Most are evergreen with needle-like leaves.

practice.q: What is the basis for classification of plants into cryptogams and phanerogams?
practice.a:
Plants are classified as cryptogams or phanerogams depending on the presence or absence of flowers, fruits and seeds.
Cryptogams (Thallophyta, Bryophyta, Pteridophyta) reproduce by spores and their reproductive organs are not visible.
Phanerogams have visible reproductive structures and produce seeds.

practice.q: Give two characteristics and two examples of Bryophyta.
practice.a:
Bryophytes grow in moist soil but need water for reproduction, hence called the 'amphibians' of the plant kingdom;
they are thalloid, multicellular, autotrophic, and lack true roots, stem and leaves.
Examples: Moss (Funaria), Marchantia.

practice.q: Distinguish between gymnosperms and angiosperms (any two points).
practice.a:
Gymnosperms bear seeds that are not enclosed in a fruit (naked seeds), e.g.
Cycas, Pinus.
Angiosperms bear flowers which develop into fruits, and their seeds are enclosed within the fruit, e.g. mango, mustard.

practice.q: What are algae? Give two examples.
practice.a:
Algae are plants of division Thallophyta that grow mainly in water, lack specific parts like root-stem-leaves-flowers, but are autotrophic due to chlorophyll.
Examples: Spirogyra, Ulothrix.

practice.q: Distinguish between monocotyledonous and dicotyledonous plants (any two points).
practice.a:
Dicotyledonous plants have seeds with two cotyledons, a well-developed tap root system, and reticulate leaf venation, e.g. banyan tree.
Monocotyledonous plants have seeds with a single cotyledon, fibrous roots, and parallel leaf venation, e.g. maize.

practice.q: Explain the characteristics of Pteridophyta with examples.
practice.a:
Plants of division Pteridophyta have well-developed roots, stem and leaves, with separate conducting tissues for water and food, but they do not bear flowers or fruits.
They reproduce with spores formed on the back surface of their leaves (asexually) and by zygote formation (sexually), and have a well-developed conducting system.
Examples include ferns like Nephrolepis, Marsilea, Pteris and Adiantum.

practice.q: Give the criteria used for classification of the plant kingdom, explaining each briefly.
practice.a:
Plants are classified based on several criteria: (1) presence or absence of specialized organs like root-stem-leaves;
(2) presence or absence of separate conducting tissues (xylem and phloem) for water and food;
(3) whether the plant bears seeds, and if so, whether the seeds are enclosed within a fruit (angiosperms) or not (gymnosperms);
and (4) the number of cotyledons present in the seed, which further divides angiosperms into monocotyledonous and dicotyledonous plants.

practice.q: Write the characteristics of the subkingdom Phanerogams.
practice.a:
Phanerogams are plants that possess special structures (flowers) for reproduction and produce seeds.
After reproduction, seeds containing an embryo and stored food are formed, and this stored food nourishes the embryo during germination.
Depending on whether the seeds are enclosed in a fruit or not, phanerogams are further classified into gymnosperms (seeds not covered, e.g.
Cycas, Pinus) and angiosperms (seeds covered within fruit, e.g. mustard, mango), the latter being further divided into monocotyledons and dicotyledons.

practice.q: Describe the classification of the Kingdom Plantae with a labelled flow chart, explaining each division briefly.
practice.a:
Kingdom Plantae is first divided into Cryptogams (reproduce by spores) and Phanerogams (produce seeds).
Cryptogams are further divided into: (1) Thallophyta – plants without differentiated root-stem-leaf, mainly aquatic, e.g.
Spirogyra, algae;
(2) Bryophyta – 'amphibians of the plant kingdom', need water for reproduction, e.g.
Moss, Marchantia;
(3) Pteridophyta – have well-developed roots, stem, leaves and conducting tissue but no flowers/fruits, reproduce by spores, e.g. ferns.
Phanerogams are divided into: (1) Gymnosperms – seeds not enclosed in fruit, e.g.
Cycas, Pinus;
(2) Angiosperms – seeds enclosed in fruit, further divided into Dicotyledons (two cotyledons, tap root, reticulate venation, e.g. mustard) and Monocotyledons (one cotyledon, fibrous root, parallel venation, e.g. maize).
This classification is based on presence of specialized tissues, seed formation, and the covering and number of cotyledons in seeds.

practice.q: Sketch and describe the plant Spirogyra, mentioning its classification, habitat and reproduction.
practice.a:
Spirogyra is a green alga belonging to division Thallophyta of the plant kingdom.
It is a filamentous, multicellular, autotrophic plant found commonly in fresh, stagnant water such as ponds.
Its cells are cylindrical and arranged end to end to form long unbranched filaments.
Each cell has a cell wall, cytoplasm, and one or more spirally arranged ribbon-like chloroplasts (which give the plant its characteristic spiral appearance under the microscope), along with pyrenoids (starch-storing bodies) and a nucleus.
Spirogyra lacks true root, stem and leaves, and does not have specialised conducting tissue.
It reproduces asexually by fragmentation of the filament and sexually by a process called conjugation, in which the contents of two cells from adjacent filaments fuse to form a zygospore.
Being a member of Thallophyta, it is classified among the cryptogams as its reproductive structures are not visibly differentiated.

chapters.title: Energy Flow in an Ecosystem
notes: Ecosystem: A functional unit where living organisms interact with one another and with their physical environment. Includes biotic and abiotic components.
Biotic Components: Living organisms in an ecosystem — producers, consumers and decomposers.
Abiotic Components: Non-living parts of an ecosystem such as air, water, sunlight, soil and temperature.
Producers: Green plants that prepare their own food by photosynthesis. They form the first trophic level and are the primary source of energy.
Consumers: Organisms that depend directly or indirectly on producers for food. Classified into primary, secondary and tertiary consumers.
Primary Consumers: Herbivores that feed directly on plants. Examples include deer, rabbits, grasshoppers and cows.
Secondary Consumers: Carnivores or omnivores that feed on primary consumers. They occupy the third trophic level.
Tertiary Consumers: Top carnivores that feed on secondary consumers. Found at the highest trophic level.
Decomposers: Organisms such as bacteria and fungi that break down dead plants and animals into simpler substances. They recycle nutrients back into the environment.
Food Chain: A sequence in which energy passes from one organism to another through feeding relationships. Always begins with producers.
Types of Food Chain: Grazing food chain and detritus food chain both help in the transfer of energy and nutrients.
Food Web: A network of interconnected food chains in an ecosystem. Provides multiple feeding relationships and increases ecosystem stability.
Trophic Levels: Each stage in a food chain. Energy decreases as it moves from one trophic level to the next.
Flow of Energy: Energy flows in one direction from the Sun to producers then to consumers. Unlike nutrients, energy is not recycled.
Ten Percent Law: Only about 10% of the energy at one trophic level is transferred to the next level. The remaining 90% is lost as heat.
Ecological Pyramid: A graphical representation of number, biomass or energy at different trophic levels. The pyramid of energy is always upright.
Nutrient Cycle: Nutrients move continuously between living organisms and the environment. These cycles maintain the balance of essential elements in nature.
Ecological Balance: Maintained when all living and non-living components exist in proper proportion. Disturbance in any component affects the entire ecosystem.
Conservation of Ecosystems: Protecting forests, reducing pollution and conserving biodiversity help maintain healthy ecosystems.

flashcards.q: What is an ecosystem?
flashcards.a: A functional unit where living organisms interact with one another and with their physical environment.

flashcards.q: Who are producers in an ecosystem?
flashcards.a: Producers are green plants that prepare their own food by photosynthesis.

flashcards.q: What is a food chain?
flashcards.a: A sequence through which energy passes from one organism to another by feeding, starting from producers.

flashcards.q: State the Ten Percent Law.
flashcards.a: Only about 10% of the energy at one trophic level is transferred to the next trophic level.

flashcards.q: What is the role of decomposers in an ecosystem?
flashcards.a: Decomposers break down dead organisms and recycle nutrients back into the environment.

flashcards.q: What is a food web?
flashcards.a: A network of interconnected food chains in an ecosystem that provides multiple feeding relationships.

flashcards.q: What is an ecological pyramid?
flashcards.a: A graphical representation of number, biomass or energy at different trophic levels of an ecosystem.

practice.q: What is a food chain?
practice.a: A food chain is the definite sequence of interactions between producers, consumers and saprophytes/decomposers in an ecosystem, showing the transfer of food energy from one organism to another, e.g. grass → grasshopper → frog → snake.

practice.q: What is meant by a trophic level?
practice.a:
A trophic level is the step or stage at which an organism obtains its food in a food chain.
Producers form the first trophic level, herbivores the second, carnivores the third, and so on;
the amount of energy decreases at each successive trophic level.

practice.q: Define the bio-geo-chemical cycle.
practice.a:
The cyclical flow of nutrients between biotic (living) and abiotic (non-living) components of an ecosystem, through the biosphere formed by the lithosphere, atmosphere and hydrosphere, is called the bio-geo-chemical cycle.
Examples include the carbon cycle, oxygen cycle and nitrogen cycle.

practice.q: Why is the flow of energy in an ecosystem said to be 'one way'?
practice.a:
Energy flows from the sun to producers (plants), then to herbivores, carnivores and decomposers, and is dissipated as heat at each step;
no part of this energy is ever returned back to the sun.
Hence, unlike nutrients (which cycle), energy flow through an ecosystem is unidirectional, or 'one way'.

practice.q: What is meant by nitrogen fixation?
practice.a: Nitrogen fixation is the process of converting atmospheric (free) nitrogen gas into usable compounds like nitrates and nitrites, through atmospheric, industrial or biological processes (such as the action of nitrogen-fixing bacteria).

practice.q: Explain the difference between a food chain and a food web.
practice.a:
A food chain is a single, linear sequence showing the transfer of food energy from producers through various levels of consumers, e.g. grass → deer → tiger.
A food web is formed when many interconnected food chains overlap in an ecosystem, since an organism may be the prey of, or feed on, several different organisms;
hence a food web is a complex, interlinked network rather than a single straight line, and it is more commonly found in nature since organisms usually have multiple food sources.

practice.q: Explain the pyramid of energy in an ecosystem.
practice.a:
The pyramid of energy represents the amount of energy present at each trophic level of a food chain, with producers at the base and top (apex) consumers at the peak.
The quantity of energy (and the number of organisms) decreases progressively from the producer level to the top consumer level, because energy is lost as heat during metabolic processes at each transfer.
This pattern of decreasing energy transfer through successive trophic levels is called the pyramid of energy, first proposed by the British scientist Charles Elton in 1927 (hence also called the Eltonian pyramid).

practice.q: Explain any three important processes involved in the nitrogen cycle.
practice.a:
1.
Nitrogen fixation: conversion of atmospheric nitrogen gas into nitrates and nitrites through atmospheric, industrial or biological processes (e.g. by nitrogen-fixing bacteria). 2.
Ammonification: release of ammonia through the decomposition of dead organisms and excretory wastes by decomposer microbes. 3.
Nitrification: conversion of ammonia into nitrites and then into nitrates by nitrifying bacteria, making nitrogen available for absorption by plants.

practice.q: Explain the carbon cycle in detail with the help of a diagram/description, mentioning the main biotic and abiotic processes involved.
practice.a:
The carbon cycle is the continuous circulation and recycling of carbon between the atmosphere and living organisms, and back to the atmosphere after their death.
Plants absorb carbon dioxide from the atmosphere and convert it into carbohydrates and other carbon compounds (proteins, fats) through photosynthesis (6CO2 + 12H2O → C6H12O6 + 6H2O + 6O2, using sunlight and chlorophyll).
Herbivores obtain this biotic carbon by feeding on plants, and carnivores obtain it by feeding on herbivores, thus passing carbon along the food chain up to apex consumers.
All organisms release carbon dioxide back into the atmosphere through respiration (C6H12O6 + 6O2 → 6CO2 + 6H2O + Energy).
After death, decomposers (bacteria and fungi) break down the bodies of all organisms, releasing carbon dioxide back into the atmosphere for reuse by plants.
Abiotic processes such as combustion of fossil fuels and wood, forest fires, and volcanic activity also release carbon dioxide into the atmosphere.
Since carbon moves rapidly between the atmosphere and living organisms compared to its long-term storage in coal, limestone, or petroleum, the carbon cycle is one of the fastest and most important bio-geo-chemical cycles, and its balance is essential for maintaining stable atmospheric carbon dioxide levels.

practice.q: Explain the inter-relationship between the food chain and food web, and discuss why equilibrium is necessary in a food web.
practice.a:
A food chain is a single sequence of feeding relationships (producer → primary consumer → secondary consumer → apex consumer), while in nature, an ecosystem contains many interconnected food chains, since most organisms feed on more than one type of food source and are, in turn, eaten by more than one predator.
When such multiple, interlinked food chains are combined, they form an intricate network called a food web.
Thus, a food web can be thought of as a combination of many overlapping food chains, and understanding food webs gives a more realistic picture of energy and matter flow in an ecosystem than a single food chain does.
Equilibrium (balance) is necessary in a food web because if the population of any one organism (say, a producer or a particular consumer) increases or decreases drastically, it disturbs the food supply and population of all the organisms dependent on it at other trophic levels, potentially causing a chain reaction that could destabilize the whole ecosystem.
For example, if herbivores are eliminated, carnivores dependent on them would starve, while if producers decline, the entire food web collapses.
Hence maintaining a balance between the various populations at different trophic levels within a food web is essential for the stability and survival of the ecosystem.

chapters.title: Useful and Harmful Microbes
notes: Microorganisms: Tiny living organisms that cannot be seen with the naked eye. Found in air, water, soil and inside living organisms.
Types of Microorganisms: Bacteria, fungi, protozoa, algae and viruses — each group has its own characteristics.
Bacteria: Unicellular organisms without a true nucleus. Reproduce rapidly by binary fission and may be useful or harmful.
Fungi: Eukaryotic microorganisms that absorb food from dead and decaying organic matter. Reproduce by spores and are important decomposers.
Protozoa: Single-celled organisms with a well-defined nucleus. Some are free-living; others cause diseases in humans and animals.
Algae: Chlorophyll-containing organisms that prepare food through photosynthesis. Produce oxygen and serve as food for aquatic organisms.
Viruses: Extremely small particles that reproduce only inside living host cells. Contain either DNA or RNA within a protein coat.
Useful Microorganisms in Food: Used to prepare curd, bread, cheese and fermented products. Yeast is used in baking; bacteria help make curd.
Microorganisms in Medicine: Certain bacteria and fungi produce antibiotics. Vaccines prepared using microorganisms protect against infectious diseases.
Microorganisms in Agriculture: Some bacteria fix atmospheric nitrogen into soil, improving crop productivity and reducing need for chemical fertilizers.
Decomposition: Bacteria and fungi break down dead plants and animals into simpler substances, recycling nutrients and keeping the environment clean.
Nitrogen Cycle: A natural process in which nitrogen is continuously recycled between the atmosphere, soil and living organisms.
Harmful Microorganisms: Some cause diseases in humans, animals and plants. They may also spoil food and damage useful materials.
Food Spoilage: Microorganisms grow on food under favourable conditions and cause it to spoil. Proper storage and preservation help prevent this.
Food Preservation: Process of preventing food from spoiling by controlling growth of microorganisms. Methods include drying, refrigeration, salting and pasteurization.
Diseases Caused by Bacteria: Cholera, typhoid, tuberculosis and pneumonia.
Diseases Caused by Viruses: Influenza, polio, measles, dengue and AIDS.
Diseases Caused by Protozoa: Malaria and amoebic dysentery — spread through contaminated food, water or insect vectors.
Prevention of Microbial Diseases: Personal hygiene, safe water, proper sanitation and vaccination help prevent many microbial diseases.
Importance of Vaccination: Vaccination prepares the body's immune system to fight disease-causing microorganisms.

flashcards.q: What are microorganisms?
flashcards.a: Microorganisms are tiny living organisms that can be seen only with the help of a microscope.

flashcards.q: Name the five main groups of microorganisms.
flashcards.a: Bacteria, fungi, protozoa, algae and viruses.

flashcards.q: How are microorganisms useful in agriculture?
flashcards.a: Some microorganisms fix atmospheric nitrogen in the soil and improve soil fertility.

flashcards.q: What is food preservation?
flashcards.a: Food preservation is the process of preventing food spoilage by controlling the growth of microorganisms.

flashcards.q: Why is vaccination important?
flashcards.a: Vaccination helps the body develop immunity and protects it from many infectious diseases.

flashcards.q: Name two diseases caused by viruses.
flashcards.a: Influenza and polio.

flashcards.q: What is the nitrogen cycle?
flashcards.a: A natural process where nitrogen is continuously recycled between the atmosphere, soil and living organisms.

practice.q: What is fermentation? Give one example.
practice.a:
Fermentation is a process brought about by microbes (such as bacteria or yeast) in which they convert sugars into other substances, such as acids or alcohol, in order to obtain energy.
For example, lactobacilli convert lactose (milk sugar) into lactic acid, causing milk to turn into yoghurt.

practice.q: What is symbiosis? Give an example.
practice.a:
Symbiosis is a relationship between two organisms in which both benefit from living together.
For example, Rhizobium bacteria living in the root nodules of leguminous plants supply the plant with nitrogenous compounds, while in return the plant supplies the bacteria with carbohydrates (energy).

practice.q: What are antibiotics? Give one example.
practice.a:
Antibiotics are substances obtained from certain bacteria and fungi that destroy or prevent the growth of harmful micro-organisms, mainly used to treat bacterial infections.
Example: Penicillin, obtained from the fungus Penicillium.

practice.q: Distinguish between broad-spectrum and narrow-spectrum antibiotics.
practice.a:
Broad-spectrum antibiotics are effective against a wide variety of bacteria and are used when the specific pathogen causing a disease is not identified, e.g. ampicillin, tetracycline.
Narrow-spectrum antibiotics are used when the specific pathogen is definitely known, e.g. penicillin, gentamycin.

practice.q: What is budding? Name the organism which reproduces by this method.
practice.a:
Budding is a method of asexual reproduction in which a small outgrowth (bud) develops on the parent cell and gradually separates to form a new daughter cell/organism.
Yeast, a unicellular fungus, reproduces by budding.

practice.q: Explain the uses of Lactobacilli in daily life.
practice.a:
Lactobacilli, anaerobic bacteria found in fermented milk products, convert lactose into lactic acid (fermentation), causing coagulation of milk proteins.
Their uses include: (1) production of various milk products like yoghurt, buttermilk, cheese and shrikhand;
(2) fermentation of vegetables for pickles and production of cider, cocoa;
(3) treatment of abdominal discomfort along with other useful microbes;
(4) preparation of leavened cattle fodder;
and (5) production of wine and certain types of bread.

practice.q: Explain the role and importance of Rhizobium bacteria in agriculture.
practice.a:
Rhizobium bacteria live symbiotically in the root nodules of leguminous plants (like beans, soyabean, groundnut) and fix atmospheric nitrogen into nitrates, nitrites and amino acids, which are supplied to the host plant;
in exchange, they receive carbohydrates (energy) from the plant.
Because of this nitrogen fixation, leguminous crops are rich in proteins.
The use of Rhizobium in agriculture reduces the need for chemical fertilizers, lowering costs for farmers and reducing the adverse environmental effects of excessive fertilizer use.
Nowadays, seeds are often coated with rhizobial solution/powder before sowing (rhizobial inoculation) to boost nitrogen supply not just for legumes but for other crops as well.

practice.q: Explain the harmful effects of the bacterium Clostridium, and describe one preventive measure against food poisoning caused by it.
practice.a:
Clostridium bacteria are rod-shaped, anaerobic bacteria (cannot survive in normal atmospheric oxygen levels) that spoil cooked food, especially during large gatherings/community feasts, causing food poisoning.
These bacteria produce bottle-shaped endospores under adverse conditions and release toxins in food that can cause serious illness if consumed.
Preventive measures include proper cooking and reheating of food at adequate temperatures, avoiding storage of cooked food for long periods at room temperature, maintaining hygiene while preparing and serving food, and ensuring food is not left exposed to contamination for long durations before consumption.

practice.q: Describe how yeast is used industrially for the production of alcohol and bread, explaining the underlying biological process.
practice.a:
Yeast (Saccharomyces species) is a unicellular, heterotrophic fungus that obtains nutrition from carbon compounds like sugars.
When yeast is grown in a sugar solution, it multiplies rapidly and converts the sugar (carbohydrates) present into ethanol (alcohol) and carbon dioxide gas, through the process called fermentation.
This is exploited industrially in two major ways: (1) In alcohol/wine production, molasses (a by-product rich in carbohydrates from sugarcane processing) or grape juice (containing glucose and fructose) is fermented using yeast, producing ethanol as the primary product, which can then be processed into spirits or used directly as wine;
ethanol is also used as a smokeless, high-quality fuel, and is blended with petrol/diesel in some countries. (2) In bread-making, yeast is mixed with flour dough containing sugar;
as the yeast ferments the sugars, it releases carbon dioxide gas, which gets trapped in the dough as small bubbles, causing the dough to rise and become light, spongy and porous when baked, unlike unleavened dough (like chapati dough) which lacks this fermentation step and therefore does not become spongy.
Yeast is thus of great commercial importance in the food, beverage and biofuel industries.

practice.q: Discuss the importance of antibiotics in medicine, explaining the discovery of penicillin, and list precautions to be taken while using antibiotics.
practice.a:
Antibiotics are carbon compounds obtained from certain bacteria and fungi that destroy or inhibit the growth of harmful micro-organisms, mainly bacteria.
Their discovery in the 20th century revolutionized medicine, helping to bring diseases like tuberculosis under control in many countries.
Penicillin, the first antibiotic, was discovered accidentally in 1928 by Alexander Fleming, a professor of microbiology at St Mary's Hospital.
While studying cultures of Staphylococci bacteria in Petri dishes, he noticed that in one dish, a fungal colony (later identified as Penicillium) had grown, and the area around it was clear of bacteria — the bacteria had been destroyed by a secretion from the fungus.
Further research confirmed this secretion (penicillin) killed the bacteria, laying the foundation for antibiotic-based treatment of previously incurable bacterial infections such as pneumonia, scarlet fever, and ear/throat/skin infections.
Precautions while using antibiotics: (1) take antibiotics only when prescribed by a doctor;
(2) never purchase or self-medicate with antibiotics without a prescription;
(3) do not use antibiotics for common viral illnesses like cold or influenza;
(4) always complete the full prescribed course, even if you feel better before finishing it, to prevent bacteria developing resistance;
(5) never share your prescribed antibiotics with others, since the same antibiotic may not suit their condition and misuse promotes antibiotic resistance.

chapters.title: Environmental Management
notes: Environment: Includes all the living and non-living things that surround us. Provides resources necessary for survival of all living organisms.
Environmental Management: Proper use, protection and conservation of natural resources to maintain ecological balance and support sustainable development.
Natural Resources: Materials obtained from nature that are useful to humans — air, water, soil, forests, minerals and wildlife.
Conservation of Natural Resources: Using natural resources wisely without wasting them to ensure availability for future generations.
Biodiversity: The variety of plants, animals and microorganisms found on Earth. Greater biodiversity helps maintain stable and healthy ecosystems.
Importance of Biodiversity: Provides food, medicine, raw materials and ecological stability. Helps maintain the balance of nature.
Pollution: Contamination of the environment by harmful substances. Affects health of living organisms and damages natural ecosystems.
Air Pollution: Caused by harmful gases, smoke and dust in the atmosphere. Leads to respiratory diseases, global warming and acid rain.
Water Pollution: Harmful chemicals, sewage or industrial waste in water bodies. Makes water unsafe for drinking and aquatic life.
Soil Pollution: Caused by excessive use of fertilizers, pesticides and improper waste disposal. Reduces soil fertility and crop production.
Noise Pollution: Excessive and unwanted sound from vehicles, industries and loudspeakers. Leads to stress, hearing problems and reduced concentration.
Solid Waste Management: Proper collection, segregation, recycling and disposal of waste materials to keep the environment clean.
Biodegradable Waste: Waste that can be broken down naturally by microorganisms. Examples: food waste, paper and dry leaves.
Non-biodegradable Waste: Waste that does not decompose naturally and remains for a long time. Examples: plastic, glass and metal.
Waste Segregation: Separating waste into biodegradable and non-biodegradable categories before disposal to improve recycling.
The 3Rs: Reduce, Reuse and Recycle — following these principles helps conserve resources and minimize waste generation.
Rainwater Harvesting: Collecting and storing rainwater for future use. Helps conserve water and recharge groundwater.
Afforestation: Planting trees in areas where forests did not previously exist. Increases green cover and reduces environmental problems.
Deforestation: Large-scale cutting of forests for agriculture, industries and development. Leads to soil erosion, loss of biodiversity and climate change.
Sustainable Development: Meeting present needs without affecting the ability of future generations to meet their own needs.

flashcards.q: What is environmental management?
flashcards.a: The proper use and conservation of natural resources to protect the environment.

flashcards.q: What are biodegradable wastes?
flashcards.a: Wastes that can be decomposed naturally by microorganisms, such as food waste and paper.

flashcards.q: What are the 3Rs of waste management?
flashcards.a: Reduce, Reuse and Recycle.

flashcards.q: What is sustainable development?
flashcards.a: Meeting present needs without harming the ability of future generations to meet their own needs.

flashcards.q: What is rainwater harvesting?
flashcards.a: The process of collecting and storing rainwater for future use.

flashcards.q: What is deforestation and what are its effects?
flashcards.a: Large-scale cutting of forests. Effects include soil erosion, loss of biodiversity and climate change.

flashcards.q: What is afforestation?
flashcards.a: Planting trees in areas where forests did not previously exist to increase green cover.

practice.q: Distinguish between weather and climate.
practice.a:
