Weather refers to the atmospheric conditions at a specific time and place, which can change quickly.
Climate is the average of weather-related parameters recorded over many years for a region;
it represents the long-term predominant atmospheric condition and changes only slowly.

practice.q: What is meant by meteorology?
practice.a:
Meteorology is the science that studies the inter-relationships between the various components of air, natural cycles, geological movements of the earth, and climate.
It includes the study of storms, clouds, rainfall, thunder and lightning, and is used to make weather forecasts.

practice.q: Distinguish between biodegradable and non-biodegradable waste.
practice.a:
Biodegradable (wet) waste is easily degraded by microbes, e.g. kitchen waste, fruit and vegetable peels, dung;
it can be composted to produce manure or biofuel.
Non-biodegradable (dry) waste is not easily degraded and requires a long time and special techniques for disposal, e.g. plastic, metal.

practice.q: State any two principles of solid waste management.
practice.a:
Reduce: restricting the use of resources to avoid wastage of materials.
Reuse: using materials again for the same or a different purpose after their first use, instead of discarding them. (Other principles include Recycle, Refuse, Rethink, Research, Regulation.)

practice.q: What is the main objective of first aid?
practice.a: The main objective of first aid is to prevent death, prevent deterioration of the victim's health, and begin the process of rehabilitation, by providing immediate emergency care to a disaster/accident victim before professional medical help is available.

practice.q: Explain the importance of weather and climate in our daily life (any three points).
practice.a:
1.
Climate directly influences our basic needs — food, clothing and shelter, as well as occupations of people in a region (e.g. distinct lifestyles of Kashmiri and Rajasthani people). 2.
Climatic factors like wind speed/direction and temperature must be considered while constructing runways, seaports, bridges and skyscrapers. 3.
Weather and climate influence agricultural production, which is why meteorological study is especially important for an agrarian country like India, helping farmers plan sowing and harvesting.

practice.q: Explain the classification of solid waste with examples of any three categories.
practice.a:
Solid waste is classified based on its source: (1) Domestic waste – waste food, paper, plastic bags, vegetable peels generated from households;
(2) Industrial waste – chemicals, pigments, sludge, ash, metals generated from factories;
(3) Biomedical waste – bandages, needles, gloves, expired medicines from hospitals and clinics;
other categories include hazardous waste, farm waste, electronic waste, and radioactive waste, each requiring specific disposal methods due to differing environmental and health hazards.

practice.q: Describe the basic principles of first aid represented by the term 'ABC'.
practice.a:
A – Airway: if the victim has difficulty breathing, tilt the head backward or raise the chin to keep the respiratory passage open.
B – Breathing: if breathing has stopped, give artificial ventilation through mouth-to-mouth resuscitation.
C – Circulation: if the victim is unconscious, after giving mouth-to-mouth respiration twice, press down hard on the chest with both palms about 15 times, repeating this cycle;
this is called cardio-pulmonary resuscitation (CPR) and helps restore blood circulation.

practice.q: Explain in detail the necessity of solid waste management and describe any four of the '7 principles of solid waste management' (7 R's).
practice.a:
Solid waste management is essential because: (1) it prevents environmental pollution (air, water and soil) and keeps surroundings clean and healthy;
(2) it can generate energy (biogas, bioelectricity) and fertilizer (compost) from waste, creating employment opportunities;
(3) it reduces the strain on natural resources by promoting recycling and reuse;
(4) it improves public health and quality of life and helps maintain ecological balance;
improperly managed solid waste has caused calamities (e.g. contributing to the 2005 Mumbai flood).
The 7 principles are: Refuse – refusing to use non-degradable materials like plastic and thermocol;
Reduce – restricting use of resources and avoiding 'use and throw' items;
Reuse – using materials again for the same or another purpose instead of discarding them;
Recycle – converting waste materials like paper and glass into new useful products;
Rethink – reconsidering our habits and their environmental consequences;
Research – researching new ways to reuse materials that are temporarily unused;
and Regulation – following laws related to waste management and encouraging others to do so.
Following these principles collectively reduces the volume of waste generated, promotes resource conservation, and helps build sustainable communities.

practice.q: Explain the relationship between weather forecasting and disaster management, describing the role of the India Meteorological Department.
practice.a:
Weather forecasting and disaster management are closely related because accurate, timely predictions of weather events (like cyclones, heavy rainfall, dust storms, heat/cold waves, tsunamis) allow authorities and citizens to take precautionary and preventive measures well in advance, thereby reducing loss of life and property.
The India Meteorological Department (IMD), founded in 1875, prepares daily weather maps (published twice every 24 hours) and conducts research on climate instruments, radar and satellite-based predictions, and seismology-related forecasting.
IMD communicates crucial predictions about calamities like heavy rainfall, hot/cold waves, dust storms, and tsunamis to various government departments, mass media, and citizens, enabling early warnings and evacuation if needed.
It also provides essential weather information to sectors such as aviation, shipping, agriculture, irrigation and marine exploration, whose operations depend heavily on weather conditions.
Modern forecasting uses mathematical (dynamic) models analysed by supercomputers, statistical models comparing current data with historical patterns, and a holistic model combining the best-performing parameters from various models, to make monsoon and other predictions as reliable as possible.
Thus, meteorology (weather science) forms the scientific backbone of effective disaster preparedness and management, helping minimize the impact of natural calamities on society.

chapters.title: Information Communication Technology (ICT)
notes: Information and Communication Technology (ICT): The use of computers and communication systems to collect, process, store and share information. Important in education, business and daily life.
Information: Processed and meaningful data that helps in decision-making. Accurate information is essential for communication and problem-solving.
Communication: The process of exchanging information between people or devices. Modern communication has become faster and more efficient through ICT.
Computer: An electronic device that accepts data, processes it and produces useful information. Performs calculations quickly and stores large amounts of data.
Hardware: The physical parts of a computer that can be seen and touched. Examples: monitor, keyboard, mouse, CPU and printer.
Software: A set of programs and instructions that tell a computer how to perform tasks. Two types: system software and application software.
Input Devices: Used to enter data and instructions into a computer. Examples: keyboard, mouse, scanner and microphone.
Output Devices: Display or produce processed information from a computer. Examples: monitor, printer, speakers and projector.
Storage Devices: Used to store data and programs. Examples: hard disks, pen drives and memory cards.
Internet: A worldwide network that connects millions of computers. Allows users to access information and communicate from anywhere.
World Wide Web (WWW): A collection of web pages available through the Internet. Accessed using web browsers.
E-mail: An electronic method of sending and receiving messages over the Internet. One of the fastest communication tools.
Search Engine: A software service used to find information on the Internet. Helps users locate websites, images, videos and documents.
Digital Communication: Exchanging information using electronic devices and digital networks. Supports instant communication via emails, video calls and messaging.
Cyber Safety: Using the Internet responsibly and protecting personal information online. Strong passwords and avoiding unknown links help improve security.
Advantages of ICT: Improves communication, education, healthcare and business. Saves time, increases efficiency and provides easy access to information.
Disadvantages of ICT: Excessive dependence may lead to cybercrime, privacy issues and reduced physical activity.
Applications of ICT: Used in education, banking, healthcare, agriculture, industries and government services.
Everyday Applications: Online learning, digital banking, online shopping and video conferencing are common examples of ICT.

flashcards.q: What is Information and Communication Technology (ICT)?
flashcards.a: ICT is the use of computers and communication technologies to collect, process, store and share information.

flashcards.q: What is the Internet?
flashcards.a: The Internet is a worldwide network that connects millions of computers.

flashcards.q: What are input devices?
flashcards.a: Input devices are hardware used to enter data and instructions into a computer.

flashcards.q: What is cyber safety?
flashcards.a: Cyber safety means using the Internet responsibly and protecting personal information online.

flashcards.q: Name any two applications of ICT.
flashcards.a: ICT is used in education, healthcare, banking, agriculture and many other fields.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between hardware and software?
flashcards.a: Hardware is the physical parts of a computer. Software is the set of programs and instructions that run on the hardware.

flashcards.q: What is the World Wide Web?
flashcards.a: A collection of web pages available through the Internet, accessed using web browsers.

practice.q: What is meant by Information Communication Technology (ICT)?
practice.a:
ICT refers to communication devices and their use, as well as the services provided with their help, for producing, sharing, classifying, storing and managing information.
Examples of ICT devices include computers, mobile phones, radio and television.

practice.q: State the two main components of a computer.
practice.a:
The two main components of a computer are: (1) Hardware – all the electronic and mechanical parts of the computer;
(2) Software – the commands given to the computer, the input supplied to it, and the output (results) obtained from it.

practice.q: Distinguish between RAM and ROM.
practice.a:
RAM (Random Access Memory) is created from electronic components and can function only as long as electricity is supplied to it (volatile memory);
data can be read from and written to it.
ROM (Read Only Memory) stores information that can only be read, and this information cannot be changed (non-volatile memory).

practice.q: What is an operating system?
practice.a:
An operating system is a program that provides a means of communication between the computer and the person working on it (e.g.
DOS – Disk Operating System).
A computer cannot be used without its operating system.

practice.q: Name any two application software used in science and technology and state their use.
practice.a:
Microsoft Word – used to create documents and write equations/text material.
Microsoft Excel – used to enter numerical data in tabular form and draw various types of graphs based on that data for analysis.

practice.q: Explain the working of a computer with reference to its three main units.
practice.a:
A computer works using three main units: (1) Input unit – all data or information is entered into the computer through this unit, generally using a keyboard;
(2) Processing unit – consists of the memory unit (stores data and results), control unit (directs operations), and ALU or Arithmetic Logic Unit (performs calculations and logical operations) — together, this unit processes the input data;
(3) Output unit – the final result/solution is sent to this unit and displayed, generally through a screen/monitor or printed via a printer.

practice.q: Explain the role and importance of information communication technology in science and technology.
practice.a:
ICT plays a vital role in science and technology in several ways: (1) Demonstration – experiments and concepts (e.g. functioning of the nervous system) can be demonstrated effectively using simulation and animation software;
(2) Prediction – after compiling and processing large amounts of data, predictions can be made, e.g. in meteorology, weather forecasting relies heavily on ICT-based data analysis;
(3) Collecting scientific information – the internet, emails, video conferencing, and online databases (such as Wikipedia) allow scientists and students to access and share vast amounts of scientific information quickly and globally, keeping pace with the rapid explosion of scientific knowledge.

practice.q: Explain any three job opportunities available in the field of computers.
practice.a:
1.
Software field – includes application program development, software package development, operating system development and special-purpose scientific applications;
many companies offer jobs to develop such software. 2.
Hardware field – includes jobs in hardware designing, production, assembly, testing, and maintenance/repair of computers and related devices. 3.
Training – dedicated, competent teachers are needed to train new entrants for various computer-related jobs, making computer education/training itself a significant career field.

practice.q: Explain the different generations of computers and how scientific developments have contributed to these changes.
practice.a:
Computers have gone through five generations of development since their invention.
First generation computers (1946–1959) used large valves (vacuum tubes) for their construction;
these valves consumed a lot of electricity, generated excessive heat, and often caused the computers to shut down.
With advances in electronics, subsequent generations replaced valves with transistors (second generation), then integrated circuits (third generation), and later microprocessors (fourth generation), each innovation drastically reducing the size, heat generation and power consumption of computers while increasing their speed, reliability, and processing capability.
Today's computers belong to the fifth generation, characterized by extremely high processing speeds, artificial intelligence capabilities, and compact designs.
This rapid technological evolution — driven by advances in materials science, electronics and semiconductor technology — is what has enabled computers to enter virtually every field of work today, from scientific research to daily communication, education and industry, fundamentally transforming information communication technology.

practice.q: Explain, with suitable examples, the various devices used for information communication and how they are useful in the context of science.
practice.a:
Various devices are used for producing, sharing, storing and managing information as part of ICT.
The computer/laptop is the most versatile device, used for data processing, calculations, simulations, creating documents/presentations, and accessing the internet for scientific research;
it is essential in nearly every scientific field today.
The mobile phone allows instant communication, access to scientific apps, and mobile internet for information retrieval from any location.
Radio and television are used to broadcast scientific news, educational programmes, and weather forecasts to a wide audience, including in remote areas without internet access.
In science specifically: simulation and animation software (used via computers) help demonstrate complex scientific concepts like the functioning of the nervous system or planetary motion in a way that is difficult to show with physical models alone;
the internet, emails, video conferencing and online scientific databases/journals allow scientists across the world to collaborate, share research findings instantly, and access the latest information, preventing knowledge from becoming outdated given the rapid pace of scientific advancement.
Thus, these ICT devices collectively enhance the pace, accuracy, and reach of scientific work and communication.

chapters.title: Reflection of Light
notes: Light: A form of energy that enables us to see objects. It travels in a straight line and does not require a material medium.
Luminous and Non-luminous Objects: Luminous objects produce their own light (e.g. Sun, bulb). Non-luminous objects reflect light from other sources (e.g. Moon).
Reflection of Light: The bouncing back of light into the same medium after striking a surface. Most objects are visible because they reflect light.
Laws of Reflection: (1) The angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection. (2) The incident ray, reflected ray and normal at the point of incidence all lie in the same plane.
Plane Mirror: A smooth, flat reflecting surface. Forms an erect, virtual image of the same size as the object, as far behind the mirror as the object is in front.
Image Formed by a Plane Mirror: Virtual, erect and laterally inverted. Cannot be obtained on a screen.
Lateral Inversion: The apparent reversal of the left and right sides of an image in a plane mirror. Words appear reversed in a mirror.
Spherical Mirrors: Mirrors whose reflecting surface forms part of a sphere. Two types: concave mirror and convex mirror.
Concave Mirror: Has its reflecting surface curved inward. Can produce real or virtual images depending on the position of the object.
Convex Mirror: Has its reflecting surface curved outward. Always forms a virtual, erect and diminished image.
Pole (P): The centre of the reflecting surface of a spherical mirror.
Centre of Curvature (C): The centre of the sphere of which the mirror forms a part.
Radius of Curvature: The distance between the pole and the centre of curvature.
Principal Axis: The straight line passing through the pole and centre of curvature. Used as a reference line for ray diagrams.
Principal Focus (F): The point where rays parallel to the principal axis meet or appear to meet after reflection.
Uses of Concave Mirrors: Used in headlights, shaving mirrors, torches and solar cookers.
Uses of Convex Mirrors: Used as rear-view mirrors in vehicles because they provide a wider field of view.
Multiple Reflection: Light reflected several times between two mirrors. Used in kaleidoscopes and periscopes.

flashcards.q: What is reflection of light?
flashcards.a: Reflection is the bouncing back of light after striking a surface.

flashcards.q: State the laws of reflection.
flashcards.a: Angle of incidence = angle of reflection. Incident ray, reflected ray and normal lie in the same plane.

flashcards.q: What type of image is formed by a plane mirror?
flashcards.a: A plane mirror forms a virtual, erect and laterally inverted image.

flashcards.q: Why are convex mirrors used as rear-view mirrors?
flashcards.a: They provide a wider field of view and always form erect, diminished images.

flashcards.q: What is lateral inversion?
flashcards.a: The apparent interchange of the left and right sides of an image in a plane mirror.

flashcards.q: What is the principal focus of a mirror?
flashcards.a: The point where rays parallel to the principal axis meet or appear to meet after reflection.

flashcards.q: Name two uses of concave mirrors.
flashcards.a: Used in shaving mirrors and headlights of vehicles.

practice.q: Define the principal focus of a concave mirror.
practice.a:
The principal focus of a concave mirror is the point on the principal axis where incident rays parallel to the principal axis meet after reflection from the mirror.
It is denoted by F, and the distance from the pole to this point is called the focal length (f).

practice.q: State two uses of a concave mirror.
practice.a:
1.
Used in shaving/dental mirrors, where placing the face between the pole and focus gives an erect, magnified, virtual image. 2.
Used in torches and vehicle headlamps, where the light source is placed at the focus to produce a parallel beam of light.

practice.q: Why are convex mirrors used as rear-view/side mirrors in vehicles?
practice.a: Convex mirrors always form an erect, virtual, and diminished (smaller) image regardless of the object's distance, giving the driver a wider field of view of the traffic behind, which helps in seeing more of the surroundings in a small mirror.

practice.q: What is meant by lateral inversion?
practice.a: Lateral inversion is the phenomenon in which the image of an object formed by a plane mirror appears to be inverted from left to right (e.g. the letters of a word appear reversed), because the image of each point is formed behind the mirror at the same perpendicular distance from the mirror as the object.

practice.q: State the mirror formula and explain the terms used.
practice.a:
The mirror formula is 1/v + 1/u = 1/f, where v is the image distance from the pole, u is the object distance from the pole, and f is the focal length of the mirror.
This formula holds for all spherical mirrors, for all positions of the object.

practice.q: State the rules used for drawing ray diagrams for spherical mirrors.
practice.a:
Rule 1: An incident ray parallel to the principal axis, after reflection, passes through the principal focus.
Rule 2: An incident ray passing through the principal focus, after reflection, becomes parallel to the principal axis.
Rule 3: An incident ray passing through the centre of curvature, after reflection, retraces its own path back along the same line.

practice.q: Explain the nature, position and size of the image formed by a concave mirror when the object is placed between the pole and focus.
practice.a:
When an object is placed between the pole and the focus of a concave mirror, the image formed is located behind the mirror.
The image is virtual (since reflected rays only appear to meet, they do not actually converge), erect, and magnified (larger than the object).
This is the principle used in shaving mirrors and dental mirrors, where the object (face/tooth) is held close to the mirror, within its focal length.

practice.q: Explain divergence and convergence of light with reference to concave and convex mirrors.
practice.a:
A concave mirror is called a focusing/converging mirror because parallel incident rays, after reflection, converge (come together) at the principal focus;
the size of the image produced can be larger or smaller than the object depending on the object's distance from the mirror.
A convex mirror is called a dispersing/diverging mirror because parallel incident rays, after reflection, spread out (diverge) as if coming from a point behind the mirror;
images produced by a convex mirror are always smaller (diminished) than the object.

practice.q: Draw ray diagrams and describe the nature, size and position of images formed by a concave mirror for an object placed at: (1) the focus, (2) the centre of curvature, and (3) beyond the centre of curvature.
practice.a:
(1) Object at focus: Incident rays from the object, after reflection from the concave mirror, become parallel to each other (since a ray through the focus becomes parallel to the axis after reflection).
Since the reflected rays never meet, the image is formed at infinity;
it is real, inverted, and highly magnified (very large). (2) Object at the centre of curvature: The image is also formed at the centre of curvature itself.
It is real, inverted, and of the same size as the object, because rays reflected from the pole and passing through the centre of curvature retrace their path, converging exactly where the object is placed. (3) Object beyond the centre of curvature: The image is formed between the focus and the centre of curvature, on the same side as the object (in front of the mirror).
It is real, inverted, and diminished (smaller than the object).
As the object is moved further away (towards infinity), the image moves closer to the focus and becomes progressively smaller, approaching a point image at the focus when the object is at infinity.
In all three cases, since the reflected rays actually converge to form the image, it can be obtained on a screen (real image), unlike the virtual images formed when the object is placed between the pole and focus.

practice.q: Solve: An object of height 7 cm is kept at a distance of 25 cm in front of a concave mirror of focal length 15 cm. At what distance from the mirror should a screen be kept to obtain a clear image? What will be the size and nature of the image?
practice.a:
Given: height of object h1 = 7 cm, object distance u = -25 cm, focal length f = -15 cm.
Using the mirror formula: 1/v + 1/u = 1/f 1/v = 1/f - 1/u = 1/(-15) - 1/(-25) = -1/15 + 1/25 Taking LCM of 15 and 25 (=75): -5/75 + 3/75 = -2/75 So 1/v = -2/75, therefore v = -37.5 cm.
The screen should be placed 37.5 cm in front of the mirror (on the same side as the object) to obtain a clear image.
Magnification, M = -v/u = -(-37.5)/(-25) = -1.5 Height of image, h2 = M × h1 = -1.5 × 7 = -10.5 cm.
Since the image height is negative and the magnification is negative, the image is real, inverted, and magnified, with a height of 10.5 cm, formed at a distance of 37.5 cm from the mirror.

chapters.title: Study of Sound
notes: Sound: A form of energy produced due to vibrations of objects. Travels through a material medium as longitudinal waves.
Production of Sound: Sound is produced when an object vibrates. These vibrations create compressions and rarefactions in the surrounding medium.
Medium of Sound: Sound requires a material medium (solid, liquid or gas) to travel. It cannot travel through a vacuum.
Longitudinal Waves: Particles of the medium vibrate parallel to the direction of wave propagation. Sound waves in air are longitudinal waves.
Compression: A region where particles of the medium are close together, creating high pressure and high density.
Rarefaction: A region where particles of the medium are far apart, creating low pressure and low density.
Amplitude: Maximum displacement of vibrating particles from their mean position. Greater amplitude produces louder sound.
Frequency: The number of vibrations completed in one second. Measured in hertz (Hz).
Time Period: The time taken to complete one vibration. It is the reciprocal of frequency.
Wavelength: The distance between two successive compressions or two successive rarefactions. Represented by the Greek letter lambda (λ).
Speed of Sound: Depends on the nature and temperature of the medium. Travels fastest in solids and slowest in gases.
Audible Range: The human ear can hear sounds with frequencies between 20 Hz and 20,000 Hz.
Infrasonic Sound: Frequency below 20 Hz. Produced by earthquakes, elephants and some natural phenomena.
Ultrasonic Sound: Frequency above 20,000 Hz. Used in medical imaging, cleaning delicate objects and industrial testing.
Echo: The repetition of sound caused by reflection from a distant surface. Heard when reflected sound reaches the ear after a short interval.
Reverberation: Persistence of sound due to repeated reflections inside a closed space. Excessive reverberation reduces clarity.
Applications of Ultrasound: Used for medical diagnosis, detecting cracks in metals and measuring sea depth.
Noise Pollution: Excessive and unwanted sound in the environment. Causes stress, hearing loss and sleep disturbances.
Control of Noise Pollution: Plant trees, use silencers and avoid unnecessary use of loudspeakers.

flashcards.q: What is sound?
flashcards.a: Sound is a form of energy produced due to vibrations of objects.

flashcards.q: Can sound travel through a vacuum?
flashcards.a: No. Sound requires a material medium for propagation.

flashcards.q: What is frequency?
flashcards.a: Frequency is the number of vibrations completed in one second. Unit: hertz (Hz).

flashcards.q: What is an echo?
flashcards.a: An echo is the repetition of sound caused by reflection from a distant surface.

flashcards.q: What are ultrasonic waves?
flashcards.a: Sound waves with frequencies greater than 20,000 Hz.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between compression and rarefaction?
flashcards.a: Compression is a region of high pressure where particles are close together. Rarefaction is a region of low pressure where particles are far apart.

flashcards.q: Name three applications of ultrasound.
flashcards.a: Medical imaging (sonography), detecting cracks in metals and measuring depth of the sea.

practice.q: Define an echo.
practice.a:
An echo is the repetition of the original sound heard by us due to its reflection from a hard, distant surface such as a wall, cliff or building.
To hear a distinct echo, the reflecting surface must be at a minimum distance of about 17.2 m from the source (at 22°C).

practice.q: What is meant by reverberation?
practice.a:
Reverberation is the phenomenon in which sound waves reflect multiple times from the walls, roof, and floor of a closed room/hall, causing the sound to persist and mix together, producing a continuous, often unclear sound.
This is why some auditoriums need special sound-absorbing arrangements.

practice.q: What is SONAR? State one of its uses.
practice.a:
SONAR (Sound Navigation and Ranging) is a technique used to determine the direction, distance and speed of underwater objects using ultrasonic sound waves, using a transmitter and receiver fitted on ships.
It is used to determine the depth of the sea and to detect submarines, sunken ships and icebergs.

practice.q: Distinguish between infrasound and ultrasound.
practice.a:
Infrasound is sound with a frequency lower than 20 Hz (below the audible range), e.g. sound produced by earthquakes or a pendulum.
Ultrasound is sound with a frequency greater than 20,000 Hz (20 kHz, above the audible range), e.g. sound produced/heard by bats and dolphins.

practice.q: Name the three main parts of the human ear.
practice.a:
The human ear has three main parts: (1) Outer ear (Pinna), which collects sound waves;
(2) Middle ear, which contains the eardrum that vibrates due to sound waves;
(3) Inner ear, which contains the cochlea that converts vibrations into electrical signals sent to the brain.

practice.q: Explain why the roof of a movie theatre or conference hall is curved.
practice.a:
The curved roof of a movie theatre or conference hall is designed based on the principles of reflection of sound.
The curved shape (usually concave) is engineered so that sound waves reflecting off it get distributed evenly throughout the hall rather than concentrating reverberations at particular points, ensuring that sound reaches all corners of the hall with good clarity and reducing problems like excessive reverberation or dead spots (areas of poor sound reception).

practice.q: On what factors does the velocity of sound in a gaseous medium depend? Explain briefly.
practice.a:
The velocity of sound in a gas depends on: (1) Temperature (T) – velocity is directly proportional to the square root of temperature, so increasing temperature increases the velocity of sound;
(2) Density (ρ) of the gas – velocity is inversely proportional to the square root of density, so a denser gas has a lower velocity of sound;
(3) Molecular weight (M) of the gas – velocity is inversely proportional to the square root of molecular weight, so a gas with lower molecular weight (like hydrogen) has a higher velocity of sound than one with higher molecular weight (like oxygen), under the same conditions.

practice.q: Describe the structure and function of the middle ear.
practice.a:
The middle ear is a cavity containing a thin membrane called the eardrum (tympanic membrane).
When compressions in a sound wave reach the eardrum, the increased outside pressure pushes it inward;
when rarefactions reach it, the decreased pressure pulls it outward.
This continuous push-pull action causes the eardrum to vibrate in accordance with the sound wave.
These vibrations are then transmitted, via a chain of small bones, from the middle ear towards the inner ear for further processing into electrical signals.

practice.q: Explain the working of the SONAR technique with a labelled description, and describe two important uses of ultrasonic sound.
practice.a:
SONAR (Sound Navigation and Ranging) is a technique that uses ultrasonic sound waves to determine the direction, distance and speed of underwater objects.
It consists of a transmitter and a receiver, both fitted on a ship or boat.
The transmitter produces and sends ultrasonic sound waves into the water;
these waves travel through water, strike underwater objects (like the seabed, submarines or sunken ships), and get reflected back.
The reflected waves are picked up by the receiver, which converts the ultrasonic sound into electrical signals;
these signals are then analysed, and the time difference between transmission and reception of the wave is measured.
Using this time difference and the known velocity of sound in water, the distance of the reflecting object from the ship can be calculated (distance = velocity × time/2, since the wave travels to the object and back).
SONAR is used to determine the depth of the sea, and to locate underwater hills, valleys, submarines, icebergs and sunken ships.
Two important uses of ultrasonic sound: (1) Sonography – ultrasonic waves are used to generate images of internal organs of the human body, useful for diagnosing the cause of pain, swelling or infection, studying the condition of the heart, and monitoring the growth of a foetus in a pregnant woman, all without invasive surgery. (2) Sterilization and cleaning – ultrasonic sound is used to sterilize liquids like milk by killing bacteria (extending shelf life), and in industry, to clean intricate machine parts that are otherwise inaccessible to hands, as well as to detect cracks and faults in metal blocks.

practice.q: Solve: Sunil is standing between two parallel walls. The wall closest to him is at a distance of 660 m. If he shouts, he hears the first echo after 4 seconds and another echo after 2 more seconds. Find (a) the velocity of sound in air and (b) the distance between the two walls.
practice.a:
Given: Distance to nearer wall = 660 m, time for first echo = 4 s. (a) The sound travels to the nearer wall and back (a total distance of 2 × 660 = 1320 m) in 4 seconds.
Velocity = Distance/Time = 1320/4 = 330 m/s. (b) The second echo is heard after a further 2 seconds, i.e. at a total time of 4 + 2 = 6 s from the shout, and this echo is from the farther wall.
Total distance travelled by sound to reach the farther wall and return = velocity × total time = 330 × 6 = 1980 m.
So the distance to the farther wall = 1980/2 = 990 m.
Distance between the two walls = distance to farther wall - distance to nearer wall would only apply if both walls were on the same side;
here Sunil stands between them, so distance between the two walls = 660 + 990 = 1650 m.
Thus, the velocity of sound in air is 330 m/s, and the distance between the two walls is 1650 m.

chapters.title: Carbon – An Important Element
notes: Carbon: A non-metallic element that forms the basis of most organic compounds. Found in living organisms, fuels and many natural substances.
Properties of Carbon: Carbon is tetravalent — it forms four covalent bonds with other atoms. This allows it to form a large number of stable compounds.
Catenation: The property of carbon atoms to bond with one another and form long chains, branched chains or rings. This results in millions of carbon compounds.
Allotropes of Carbon: Different forms of the same element with different structures and properties. Diamond, graphite and fullerene are the main allotropes.
Diamond: The hardest naturally occurring substance made of carbon atoms. Used in jewellery and cutting or drilling tools.
Graphite: A soft, black allotrope of carbon with layers of atoms. Good conductor of electricity. Used in pencils, lubricants and electrodes.
Fullerene: A spherical allotrope of carbon with atoms arranged in a cage-like structure. Used in nanotechnology and medicine.
Organic Compounds: Compounds that mainly contain carbon and hydrogen. Form the basis of food, fuels, medicines and many industrial products.
Hydrocarbons: Organic compounds made only of carbon and hydrogen atoms. Main components of fuels such as petrol, diesel and natural gas.
Saturated Hydrocarbons: Contain only single covalent bonds between carbon atoms. Called alkanes and are generally less reactive.
Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Contain one or more double or triple bonds between carbon atoms. Classified as alkenes and alkynes.
Covalent Bond: Formed when two atoms share electrons with each other. Carbon mainly forms covalent compounds.
Properties of Covalent Compounds: Generally have low melting and boiling points. Most do not conduct electricity because they lack free ions.
Fuels: Substances that release heat energy when they burn. Coal, petroleum, natural gas and LPG are commonly used fuels.
Fossil Fuels: Formed from remains of plants and animals buried millions of years ago. Important energy sources but non-renewable.
Uses of Carbon Compounds: Used in medicines, plastics, textiles, fertilizers and fuels.
Environmental Effects: Burning fossil fuels releases CO₂ and pollutants. Excessive use contributes to global warming and air pollution.
Conservation of Fossil Fuels: Should be used carefully as they are limited. Using renewable energy helps conserve them.

flashcards.q: Why is carbon called a tetravalent element?
flashcards.a: Carbon is called tetravalent because it forms four covalent bonds with other atoms.

flashcards.q: What is catenation?
flashcards.a: Catenation is the property of carbon atoms to bond with one another and form long chains or rings.

flashcards.q: Name any three allotropes of carbon.
flashcards.a: Diamond, graphite and fullerene.

flashcards.q: What are hydrocarbons?
flashcards.a: Hydrocarbons are compounds made only of carbon and hydrogen atoms.

flashcards.q: Why should fossil fuels be conserved?
flashcards.a: Fossil fuels are non-renewable resources and will eventually be exhausted if used excessively.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between saturated and unsaturated hydrocarbons?
flashcards.a: Saturated hydrocarbons (alkanes) have only single bonds. Unsaturated hydrocarbons (alkenes/alkynes) have double or triple bonds.

flashcards.q: What is a covalent bond?
flashcards.a: A bond formed when two atoms share electrons with each other.

practice.q: What is allotropy? Name the three crystalline allotropes of carbon.
practice.a:
Allotropy is the property by which an element occurs in more than one form in nature, with the same chemical properties but different physical properties.
The three crystalline allotropes of carbon are diamond, graphite and fullerene.

practice.q: Give two uses of graphite.
practice.a:
1.
Graphite is used in pencils for writing (as 'pencil lead', mixed with clay). 2.
Graphite is used to make carbon electrodes and lubricants, since it is a good conductor of electricity and has a slippery, layered structure.

practice.q: Define saturated and unsaturated hydrocarbons with one example each.
practice.a:
Saturated hydrocarbons have only single covalent bonds between carbon atoms, e.g. ethane (C2H6).
Unsaturated hydrocarbons have at least one multiple (double or triple) bond between carbon atoms, e.g. ethene (H2C=CH2, a double bond) or ethyne (HC≡CH, a triple bond).

practice.q: State any two uses of carbon dioxide.
practice.a:
1.
Carbon dioxide is used to make aerated (carbonated) soft drinks. 2.
Solid carbon dioxide (dry ice) is used in cold storage to keep perishable items cool during transport, and CO2-based fire extinguishers are used to put out small fires, especially involving electrical equipment, since CO2 is a non-conductor.

practice.q: What is biogas? State its main constituent.
practice.a:
Biogas is a fuel gas produced by the anaerobic (without air) decomposition of organic waste such as animal dung, dry leaves and wet garbage, by microbes in a biogas plant.
Its main constituent is methane (55-60%), with the rest mostly being carbon dioxide.

practice.q: Explain the difference between diamond and graphite in terms of structure and properties.
practice.a:
In diamond, each carbon atom is bonded to four neighbouring carbon atoms by strong covalent bonds arranged in a rigid three-dimensional structure, making diamond extremely hard, a poor conductor of electricity (no free electrons), and giving it a very high melting point.
In graphite, each carbon atom is bonded to only three other carbon atoms, forming flat hexagonal layers (sheets);
these layers can slip over each other easily (since they are held together by weak forces), making graphite soft, slippery, and a good conductor of electricity, because free electrons can move within each layer.

practice.q: Explain the different types of coal based on their formation and carbon content.
practice.a:
Coal, a fossil fuel, is formed in four progressive stages with increasing carbon content: (1) Peat – the first stage, with a high water content and less than 60% carbon, providing little heat on burning;
(2) Lignite – formed from peat under increased pressure and temperature, containing 60-70% carbon;
(3) Bituminous coal – the third stage, containing 70-90% carbon;
(4) Anthracite – the purest and hardest form of coal, containing about 95% carbon, and giving the maximum heat on combustion.

practice.q: Explain why biogas is considered an eco-friendly fuel, describing the two stages of its production.
practice.a:
Biogas is considered eco-friendly because it is produced from organic waste materials (animal dung, dry leaves, wet garbage) that would otherwise pollute the environment, and it also yields good quality manure as a by-product, reducing the need for chemical fertilizers;
being a cheap, renewable fuel, it reduces dependence on fossil fuels.
Biogas production is an anaerobic process occurring in two stages: (1) Production of acids – microbes act on the biodegradable complex organic compounds and convert them into organic acids;
(2) Methane gas production – methanogenic bacteria act on these organic acids to produce methane gas along with carbon dioxide (e.g.
CH3COOH → CH4 + CO2).

practice.q: Explain the various allotropes of carbon (crystalline and non-crystalline), giving the structure, properties and one use of each.
practice.a:
Carbon exhibits allotropy, occurring in several forms with the same chemical composition but different physical properties.
Crystalline allotropes have a regular, definite arrangement of atoms and high melting/boiling points: (1) Diamond – each carbon atom is bonded to four others by strong covalent bonds in a rigid 3D structure, making it the hardest natural substance, a bad conductor of electricity, and used in glass-cutting tools, rock-drilling machines and jewellery. (2) Graphite – each carbon atom bonds to three others, forming layered hexagonal sheets that can slip over each other, making it soft, slippery, and a good conductor of electricity (used in pencils, electrodes, and lubricants). (3) Fullerene – a rare allotrope with molecules like C60 (buckminsterfullerene), consisting of 30 to 900 carbon atoms arranged in ball-like (buckyball) or tube-like (buckytube) structures;
it is used as an insulator, as a catalyst in water purification, and exhibits superconductivity at certain temperatures.
Non-crystalline (amorphous) allotropes lack a regular atomic arrangement: coal (in its various forms — peat, lignite, bituminous coal, anthracite) and coke (obtained after removing coal gas from coal) are used as domestic and industrial fuels and as reducing agents.
Thus, despite being made of the same element, these allotropes show widely differing physical properties due to differences in their internal atomic arrangement.

practice.q: Describe the preparation, physical and chemical properties of carbon dioxide, and explain two of its important industrial/practical uses.
practice.a:
Preparation: Carbon dioxide can be prepared in the laboratory by reacting a metal carbonate (such as calcium carbonate, e.g. marble or limestone pieces) with dilute hydrochloric acid: CaCO3 + 2HCl → CaCl2 + H2O + CO2.
The gas evolved is collected by upward displacement of air, since CO2 is denser than air.
Physical properties: Carbon dioxide is a colourless, odourless gas, denser than air, and its molecular mass is 44 u;
its melting point is -56.6°C.
Chemical properties: It does not support combustion (a burning candle placed in it is extinguished);
it turns limewater milky due to the formation of insoluble calcium carbonate (Ca(OH)2 + CO2 → CaCO3 + H2O);
it dissolves in water to form a weak acid, carbonic acid, which turns blue litmus faintly red;
and it reacts with NaOH solution to form sodium carbonate (2NaOH + CO2 → Na2CO3 + H2O).
Uses: (1) Carbon dioxide is used to manufacture aerated (carbonated) drinks, as it is dissolved under pressure to give the characteristic fizz. (2) It is used in fire extinguishers — a CO2-based fire extinguisher contains sodium bicarbonate powder and dilute sulphuric acid in a glass capsule;
when the capsule is broken (by pressing the knob), the acid reacts with the bicarbonate to release CO2 gas forcefully (2NaHCO3 + H2SO4 → Na2SO4 + 2H2O + 2CO2), which smothers the fire by cutting off its oxygen supply;
such extinguishers are especially useful for electrical fires since CO2 does not conduct electricity and causes no corrosion.
Other uses include cold storage/refrigeration (dry ice) and as a solvent in eco-friendly dry cleaning.

chapters.title: Substances in Common Use
notes: Substances in Daily Life: Many substances are used in homes, industries and agriculture. These substances improve quality of life and support human activities.
Soap: A cleansing agent prepared by the reaction of fats or oils with an alkali. It removes dirt and grease by forming micelles.
Detergent: A synthetic cleansing agent that works effectively even in hard water. Widely used for washing clothes and utensils.
Difference Between Soap and Detergent: Soap does not produce much lather in hard water because it forms scum. Detergents produce good lather in both soft and hard water.
Hard Water: Contains dissolved calcium and magnesium salts. Reduces the cleaning efficiency of soap.
Soft Water: Does not contain significant calcium and magnesium salts. Forms lather easily with soap.
Baking Soda: Sodium hydrogen carbonate (NaHCO₃). Used in baking, as an antacid and in fire extinguishers.
Washing Soda: Sodium carbonate (Na₂CO₃·10H₂O). Used for cleaning, softening hard water and in manufacture of glass and soaps.
Bleaching Powder: Used for disinfecting drinking water and bleaching cotton and paper. Also used as a germicide.
Plaster of Paris: Obtained by heating gypsum. Used for making statues, casts, toys and false ceilings.
Cement: An important building material used in construction of houses, bridges and roads. Hardens when mixed with water.
Glass: Manufactured by heating silica with suitable chemicals at high temperatures. Used in windows, bottles, lab equipment and optical instruments.
Ceramics: Hard, heat-resistant materials prepared by heating clay at high temperatures. Used for tiles, pots, sanitary ware and electrical insulators.
Polymers: Large molecules formed by joining many small molecules called monomers. Plastics, nylon and polyester are common examples.
Plastics: Lightweight, durable and water-resistant materials. Excessive use causes environmental pollution.
Synthetic Fibres: Man-made fibres such as nylon, polyester and acrylic. Strong, durable and easy to maintain.
Advantages of Synthetic Materials: Lightweight, strong, inexpensive and resistant to corrosion.
Environmental Concerns: Many synthetic materials are non-biodegradable and remain in the environment for a long time.
Safe Use of Chemicals: Household chemicals should be used according to safety instructions. Proper storage prevents accidents.

flashcards.q: What is soap made of?
flashcards.a: Soap is made by the reaction of fats or oils with an alkali.

flashcards.q: Why do detergents work better than soap in hard water?
flashcards.a: Detergents do not form scum with calcium and magnesium ions in hard water, so they produce good lather and clean effectively.

flashcards.q: What is baking soda? What is its chemical name?
flashcards.a: Baking soda is sodium hydrogen carbonate (NaHCO₃). Used in baking, as an antacid and in fire extinguishers.

flashcards.q: What is Plaster of Paris and how is it prepared?
flashcards.a: Plaster of Paris is prepared by heating gypsum. It is used for casts, statues, toys and false ceilings.

flashcards.q: What are polymers?
flashcards.a: Large molecules formed by joining many small repeating molecules called monomers. Examples: plastics, nylon.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between hard water and soft water?
flashcards.a: Hard water contains dissolved calcium and magnesium salts and reduces soap lather. Soft water lacks these salts and lathers easily.

flashcards.q: Name two uses of washing soda.
flashcards.a: Used for cleaning and for softening hard water.

practice.q: State two properties and uses of sodium chloride (common salt).
practice.a:
Sodium chloride is a colourless, crystalline, neutral salt (pH 7), salty in taste, with no water of crystallization.
It is used for the production of other salts like Na2CO3 and NaHCO3, and its aqueous solution (brine) is electrolysed to produce chlorine gas, hydrogen gas, and sodium hydroxide.

practice.q: Give two uses of sodium bicarbonate (baking soda).
practice.a:
1.
It is used in baking to make bread, cake and dhokla porous and spongy, since it is basic in nature and releases CO2 gas on heating/reacting. 2.
Being basic, it is used to reduce acidity in the stomach;
it is also used to make the CO2 required in fire extinguishers.

practice.q: What is bleaching powder? State its chemical name and one use.
practice.a:
Bleaching powder is a yellowish-white solid compound with the chemical name calcium oxychloride (CaOCl2), which releases chlorine gas on reaction with carbon dioxide or dilute acids, giving it disinfecting and bleaching properties.
It is used to disinfect drinking water at water works and swimming pools.

practice.q: Name the three types of radiation emitted by radioactive substances.
practice.a: Radioactive substances emit three types of radiation: alpha (α) rays (positively charged, current of helium nuclei), beta (β) rays (negatively charged, current of electrons), and gamma (γ) rays (electrically neutral, electromagnetic radiation with the highest penetrating power).

practice.q: What is Teflon? State one property and one use.
practice.a:
Teflon is the polymer of tetrafluoroethylene (polytetrafluoroethene), discovered by Roy J.
Plunkett in 1938.
Neither water nor oil sticks to Teflon-coated surfaces, and it is unaffected by high temperatures (melting point 327°C);
it is used for making non-stick cookware.

practice.q: Explain the properties and uses of washing soda (Na2CO3).
practice.a:
Washing soda (crystalline sodium carbonate, Na2CO3.10H2O) is a whitish, odourless, hygroscopic (moisture-absorbing) compound whose aqueous solution turns litmus blue (basic in nature).
It softens hard water by converting the soluble calcium and magnesium chloride/sulphate salts present in hard water into insoluble carbonate precipitates (e.g.
MgCl2 + Na2CO3 → MgCO3 + 2NaCl), allowing soap to lather properly.
It is mainly used for washing clothes, and also in the glass industry, paper industry, and petroleum refining.

practice.q: Explain the uses and hazards of radioactive isotopes with two examples each from medicine and industry.
practice.a:
Radioactive isotopes have many beneficial uses: In medicine, phosphorus-32 is used to treat polycythemia (excess red blood cells);
iodine-123 is used to treat hyperthyroidism;
cobalt-60, boron-10 and iodine-131 are used in tumour detection and treatment.
In industry, cobalt-60 and iridium-192 are used in radiography to detect internal cracks in metal castings;
radioactive isotopes are used to measure thickness, density and levels of materials during manufacturing.
However, radioactive radiation is also hazardous: it can damage the central nervous system, cause hereditary defects by affecting DNA, cause skin cancer and leukaemia on prolonged exposure, and radioactive pollutants released into water/air can enter the food chain (e.g. through fish or milk) and accumulate in the human body, causing serious diseases.

practice.q: Explain the harmful effects of artificial food colours and dyes on human health.
practice.a:
Artificial food colours, often added to sweets, pickles, jams, sauces and soft drinks to make them attractive, sometimes contain small quantities of harmful substances like lead and mercury, which can be detrimental to health if consumed regularly.
Excessive consumption of foods with artificial colours has also been linked to conditions like ADHD (Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder) in children.
Similarly, artificial dyes, such as those used in hair colouring or the mercury-based red colour used during festivals like Rang Panchami, can cause hair fall, skin burns, eye damage, blindness, skin cancer, asthma and permanent blocking of sweat pores;
hence, natural alternatives are recommended wherever possible.

practice.q: Describe alpha, beta and gamma rays with reference to their nature, charge, penetrating power and behaviour in an electric field, and explain how these rays were discovered/differentiated by Rutherford and Willard.
practice.a:
Radioactive substances spontaneously emit three types of radiation: Alpha (α) rays are a stream of alpha particles (helium nuclei, He++), have a mass of about 4.0028 u, carry a charge of +2, travel at 1/5 to 1/20 the speed of light, and are deflected towards the negatively charged plate in an electric field;
they have very high ionizing power but very low penetrating power (stopped by an aluminium sheet less than 0.02 mm thick, or even a sheet of paper).
Beta (β) rays are a stream of high-speed electrons, have negligible mass (0.000548 u), carry a charge of -1, travel at 1/5 to 9/10 the speed of light, and are deflected towards the positively charged plate;
they have moderate penetrating power (can pass through an aluminium sheet about 2 mm thick, 100 times more penetrating than alpha rays) and lower ionizing power than alpha rays.
Gamma (γ) rays are electromagnetic radiation (like X-rays but of higher energy), have no mass and no charge, travel at the speed of light, and are not deflected at all in an electric field;
they have the highest penetrating power (can pass through a 15 cm thick lead screen, about 10,000 times the penetrating power of alpha rays) but the lowest ionizing power of the three.
These three types were differentiated by passing the radiation emitted by a radioactive substance through a strong electric field created between two oppositely charged plates, with a photographic plate placed to record where the rays landed.
Rutherford (in 1889/1902) discovered that the emitted radiation split into two types under the electric field — one deviating slightly towards the negative plate (named alpha rays) and another deviating substantially towards the positive plate (named beta rays);
Willard later discovered a third type that showed no deviation at all in the electric field (named gamma rays).
This experiment conclusively proved that radioactive substances emit three physically and chemically distinct types of radiation, differing in charge, mass and penetrating ability.

practice.q: Explain in detail the properties, uses and manufacturing process of ceramics, including porcelain, with suitable examples.
practice.a:
Ceramic is a heat-resistant substance made by kneading an inorganic material (such as clay) with water, shaping it, and then hardening it by firing (heating) at a high temperature.
The process involves: kneading clay in water, shaping the object as desired, and firing it in a kiln at 1000-1150°C, which produces a porous ceramic object.
To eliminate this porosity and give a shiny finish, the fired object is coated with a glaze (finely ground glass powder suspended in water) and fired again;
this makes the surface shiny and non-porous.
Common examples of ceramic articles include earthenware pots made by potters, roofing tiles, construction bricks, and terracotta articles.
Porcelain is a special, hard, translucent, white ceramic made using a special white clay called kaolin (found in China), mixed with glass, granite and feldspar, then kneaded with water, shaped, and fired at a higher temperature of 1200-1450°C;
after glazing and refiring, beautiful, shiny porcelain articles are obtained, which are used to make laboratory vessels and fine crockery.
Bone china, a variant of porcelain, is made by adding animal bone ash to the china clay-feldspar-silica mixture, producing a ceramic harder than ordinary porcelain.
Advanced ceramics use oxides such as alumina (Al2O3), zirconia (ZrO2) and silica (SiO2), along with compounds like silicon carbide, instead of clay, and require much higher firing temperatures (1600-1800°C) in an oxygen-free atmosphere (a process called sintering);
such advanced ceramics can withstand extremely high temperatures, are brittle but water-resistant and electrically insulating, and are used in electrical instruments, jet engine blades, kiln linings, ship exteriors, and even the outer heat-shield tiles of space shuttles, since ceramics do not decompose even under extreme thermal stress.

chapters.title: Life Processes in Living Organisms
notes: Life Processes: The basic activities performed by living organisms to maintain life — nutrition, respiration, transportation, excretion, growth and reproduction.
Nutrition: The process by which organisms obtain and utilize food for energy, growth and repair. Essential for survival.
Autotrophic Nutrition: Organisms prepare their own food using sunlight, CO₂ and water. Green plants are autotrophs performing photosynthesis.
Heterotrophic Nutrition: Organisms depend on other organisms for food. Animals, fungi and many microorganisms are heterotrophs.
Photosynthesis: Green plants prepare food in the presence of sunlight and chlorophyll. Equation: CO₂ + H₂O → Glucose + O₂ (Sunlight and Chlorophyll required).
Respiration: Breaking down food to release energy required for life activities. Takes place continuously in cells of all living organisms.
Aerobic Respiration: Occurs in the presence of oxygen and releases a large amount of energy. Produces CO₂ and water as end products.
Anaerobic Respiration: Occurs in the absence of oxygen and releases less energy. Observed in yeast and certain bacteria.
Transportation in Plants: Water and minerals transported through xylem; food is transported through phloem.
Xylem: Carries water and dissolved minerals from roots to leaves. Also provides mechanical support.
Phloem: Transports food prepared in leaves to all parts of the plant to support growth and food storage.
Transportation in Humans: The circulatory system transports oxygen, nutrients and hormones to all body parts. Also carries waste to excretory organs.
Heart: A muscular organ that pumps blood throughout the body, maintaining continuous circulation.
Blood: A connective tissue that transports oxygen, nutrients, hormones and waste materials. Protects against infections.
Excretion: The process of removing metabolic waste products from the body to maintain internal balance.
Human Excretory System: Consists of kidneys, ureters, urinary bladder and urethra. Removes nitrogenous wastes and excess water.
Transpiration: Loss of water as water vapour from aerial parts of plants. Helps cool the plant and aids upward transport of water.
Importance of Life Processes: Ensure survival, growth and reproduction. Without them, living organisms cannot sustain life.

flashcards.q: What are life processes?
flashcards.a: Basic activities that maintain life in living organisms: nutrition, respiration, transportation, excretion, growth and reproduction.

flashcards.q: What is photosynthesis?
flashcards.a: The process by which green plants prepare food using sunlight, CO₂ and water. O₂ is released as a by-product.

flashcards.q: What is the function of xylem?
flashcards.a: Xylem transports water and minerals from the roots to different parts of the plant.

flashcards.q: What is respiration?
flashcards.a: Respiration is the process of releasing energy from food in cells.

flashcards.q: What is excretion?
flashcards.a: Excretion is the process of removing metabolic waste products from the body.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between aerobic and anaerobic respiration?
flashcards.a: Aerobic respiration uses oxygen and releases more energy. Anaerobic respiration occurs without oxygen and releases less energy.

flashcards.q: What is transpiration?
flashcards.a: The loss of water in the form of water vapour from the aerial parts of plants.

practice.q: What is transpiration? Name the structures through which it occurs.
practice.a:
Transpiration is the process by which plants release excess water in the form of vapour into the atmosphere, mainly through small pores called stomata present on their leaves.
This helps in the upward movement of water and minerals through the plant and cools the plant.

practice.q: Define excretion.
practice.a:
Excretion is the process of removal of harmful and waste substances (such as urea, uric acid, ammonia) produced during the metabolic activities of an organism, from its body.
Failure to remove such wastes can lead to serious harm or even death.

practice.q: What is the functional unit of the kidney called? Name its important part.
practice.a:
The functional unit of the kidney is called a nephron;
each kidney contains about 10 lakh (1 million) nephrons.
Its important part, the cup-shaped Bowman's capsule, along with the network of capillaries within it called the glomerulus, performs the basic function of filtration of blood.

practice.q: What is a reflex action? Give one example.
practice.a:
A reflex action is an immediate, involuntary response given by the body to a stimulus from the environment, without conscious control by the brain.
Example: withdrawing the hand immediately upon accidentally touching a hot object.

practice.q: What is meant by 'tropism' or tropic movement in plants?
practice.a: Tropism (tropic movement) is the movement or growth of any part of a plant in response to an external stimulus, such as light (phototropism), gravity (gravitropism), water (hydrotropism), or specific chemicals (chemotropism).

practice.q: Explain the process of transportation of water in plants with reference to root pressure and transpiration pull.
practice.a:
Water and minerals from the soil enter root hair cells due to a difference in concentration, causing these cells to become turgid and exert pressure on adjacent cells;
this is called root pressure, and it pushes water up into the xylem, especially helping water rise during the night.
During the day, water evaporates from leaf surfaces through stomata (transpiration);
as water is lost, it creates a 'pull' that draws more water upward through the xylem from the roots to replace the lost water — this is called transpiration pull.
Together, root pressure and transpiration pull are responsible for the continuous upward movement of water and dissolved minerals throughout the plant.

practice.q: Explain the process of urine formation in the human nephron.
practice.a:
Blood containing urea (produced in the liver) enters the glomerulus (a network of capillaries within the Bowman's capsule) of the nephron under pressure, where it is filtered through the capillary walls;
water and small molecules pass through the semi-permeable membrane into the cavity of Bowman's capsule, while blood cells and large proteins are retained in the blood.
This filtered fluid then passes through the tubular part of the nephron, where useful substances (like glucose, some water, and salts) are selectively reabsorbed back into the blood.
The remaining fluid, containing waste materials like urea, forms urine, which passes through the collecting duct into the ureter, then to the urinary bladder for temporary storage, before being expelled via the urethra.

practice.q: Explain co-ordination in plants with examples of phototropic, gravitropic and chemotropic movements.
practice.a:
Since plants lack a nervous system, they show co-ordination mainly through tropic (growth-related) movements in response to external stimuli.
Phototropic movement is the growth of the shoot system towards the source of light (positive phototropism), helping the plant maximize photosynthesis.
Gravitropic movement is the growth of the root system in response to gravity (usually growing downward, positive gravitropism), anchoring the plant and helping roots reach water and minerals in the soil.
Chemotropic movement is the growth of a plant part in response to a specific chemical stimulus, e.g. the growth of the pollen tube towards the ovule, guided by chemical signals released by the ovule, which is essential for fertilization.

practice.q: Describe the structure and function of a neuron (nerve cell), and explain how a nerve impulse is transmitted from one neuron to another through a synapse.
practice.a:
A neuron (nerve cell) is the structural and functional unit of the nervous system, specially adapted to generate and conduct electro-chemical impulses.
It consists of: a cell body containing the nucleus and cytoplasm;
dendrites, numerous small branched fibres arising from the cell body that receive information/impulses from the surroundings or other neurons;
and an axon, a single long fibre (sometimes up to a metre in length) that carries the impulse away from the cell body towards its terminal end, often covered by a protective myelin sheath (formed by Schwann cells) with gaps called nodes of Ranvier that speed up impulse conduction.
When a stimulus is received at the dendrite endings, a chemical process is triggered that generates an electrical impulse;
this impulse travels from the dendrites to the cell body, then along the axon to its terminal (the axon terminal or pre-synaptic terminal).
Since neurons are not physically continuous with each other, there is a minute gap between the axon terminal of one neuron and the dendrite of the next neuron, called the synapse (synaptic cleft).
When the impulse reaches the axon terminal, it stimulates the release of certain chemical substances (neurotransmitters) stored in synaptic vesicles;
these chemicals diffuse across the synaptic cleft and bind to receptors on the post-synaptic membrane of the next neuron's dendrite, generating a new electrical impulse in that neuron.
This process repeats along a chain of neurons, ultimately conveying the impulse to muscle cells or glands, enabling a co-ordinated response.
This mechanism allows for fast but short-lived transmission of information throughout the nervous system, working alongside the slower but longer-lasting hormonal (chemical) control system to maintain overall co-ordination in the body.

practice.q: Explain chemical co-ordination (the endocrine system) in the human body, naming at least four endocrine glands, their hormones, and functions.
practice.a:
Chemical co-ordination in the human body is brought about by hormones, which are chemical substances secreted directly into the bloodstream by ductless (endocrine) glands, without any separate duct system;
since hormones travel via blood, their action is slower but longer-lasting compared to the fast, short-lived electrical impulses of the nervous system, and both systems work together to maintain the body's internal balance (homeostasis).
Some important endocrine glands are: (1) Pituitary gland, located at the base of the brain, considered the 'master gland' as it secretes hormones controlling other glands;
it secretes growth hormone (stimulates bone growth), thyroid stimulating hormone (stimulates the thyroid gland), and antidiuretic hormone (regulates water balance in the body), among others. (2) Thyroid gland, located in the neck, secretes thyroxine, which controls the body's growth and metabolic rate, and calcitonin, which regulates calcium levels in the blood. (3) Pancreas, located behind the stomach, secretes insulin (from beta cells), which lowers blood glucose by stimulating the liver to convert excess glucose into glycogen, and glucagon (from alpha cells), which raises blood glucose by converting glycogen back to glucose;
an imbalance in insulin secretion can cause diabetes. (4) Adrenal glands, located above each kidney, secrete adrenaline and nor-adrenaline, which prepare the body to respond to stress or emergency situations (the 'fight or flight' response) by stimulating the heart and increasing metabolic activity.
Thus, through the coordinated secretion of these various hormones, the endocrine system regulates vital processes such as growth, metabolism, reproduction, and the body's response to stress, working in tandem with the nervous system for overall bodily coordination.

chapters.title: Heredity and Variation
notes: Heredity: The transmission of characteristics from parents to their offspring. Ensures that many traits are passed from one generation to the next.
Variation: Differences in characteristics among individuals of the same species. Helps organisms adapt to changing environments.
Importance of Variation: Increases chances of survival of a species under changing conditions. Forms the basis of evolution through natural selection.
Genetics: The branch of biology that studies heredity and variation. Explains how traits are inherited from parents to offspring.
Gregor Mendel: Known as the Father of Genetics because of his experiments on pea plants. His work established the basic principles of inheritance.
Traits: Observable characteristics or features of an organism. May be inherited from parents or influenced by the environment.
Genes: Units of heredity present on chromosomes. Carry the information responsible for specific traits.
Chromosomes: Thread-like structures found in the nucleus of cells. Contain genes made up of DNA.
DNA (Deoxyribonucleic Acid): The genetic material that stores hereditary information. Controls development and functioning of living organisms.
Dominant Traits: Expressed even when only one copy of the gene is present. Masks the expression of the recessive trait.
Recessive Traits: Expressed only when both copies of the gene are recessive. Remains hidden in the presence of a dominant trait.
Mendel's Monohybrid Cross: Involves inheritance of one pair of contrasting traits. Mendel observed a 3:1 ratio in the F₂ generation.
Evolution: Gradual change in living organisms over many generations. Results in development of new species from earlier forms.
Fossils: Preserved remains or traces of ancient organisms found in rocks. Provide important evidence for evolution.
Adaptation: The process by which organisms develop features that help them survive in their environment.
Natural Selection: The process by which organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more successfully. Plays an important role in evolution.
Importance of Heredity: Maintains continuity among generations while allowing useful variations. Helps scientists understand inheritance and genetic disorders.
Everyday Applications: Knowledge of heredity used in agriculture, animal breeding and medicine to develop improved crops and better disease management.

flashcards.q: What is heredity?
flashcards.a: Heredity is the transmission of characteristics from parents to offspring.

flashcards.q: Who is known as the Father of Genetics?
flashcards.a: Gregor Johann Mendel is known as the Father of Genetics.

flashcards.q: What are genes?
flashcards.a: Genes are the units of heredity that carry information for specific traits.

flashcards.q: What is variation?
flashcards.a: Variation refers to the differences in characteristics among individuals of the same species.

flashcards.q: Why are fossils important?
flashcards.a: Fossils provide evidence about the evolution and history of ancient organisms.

flashcards.q: What is natural selection?
flashcards.a: The process by which organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more successfully, driving evolution.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between dominant and recessive traits?
flashcards.a: A dominant trait is expressed when one or two copies are present. A recessive trait is expressed only when two recessive copies are present.

practice.q: Define heredity.
practice.a: Heredity is the transfer of characteristics (traits) from parents to their offspring, which is why offspring resemble their parents in many respects, e.g. puppies resembling dogs, or children resembling their parents.

practice.q: What is a gene?
practice.a:
A gene is the segment of the DNA molecule that contains all the coded information necessary for the synthesis of a particular protein.
Genes are the functional units of heredity, carrying hereditary characteristics from parents to offspring.

practice.q: Distinguish between dominant and recessive characteristics, with one example.
practice.a:
A dominant characteristic is one that expresses itself in the offspring even if only one copy of its gene is present (masking the recessive trait), e.g. tallness in pea plants (T).
A recessive characteristic is one that expresses itself only when both copies of the gene for it are present (in the absence of the dominant gene), e.g. dwarfness in pea plants (t).

practice.q: What is Down syndrome? State its cause.
practice.a:
Down syndrome is a genetic disorder caused by trisomy (an extra copy) of chromosome number 21, resulting in a total of 47 chromosomes instead of the normal 46.
It is characterized by mental retardation, short stature, a short wide neck, and a shortened lifespan.

practice.q: Name the two types of persons affected in sickle-cell anaemia based on genotype.
practice.a:
1.
Sickle-cell carrier (genotype AS) – carries the gene but does not show full symptoms. 2.
Sickle-cell sufferer (genotype SS) – shows the full symptoms of the disease, as red blood cells become sickle-shaped due to abnormal haemoglobin.

practice.q: Explain the structure of the DNA molecule.
practice.a:
DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) has a double helix structure, discovered by Watson and Crick in 1953, resembling a twisted (coiled) ladder.
Each strand of the DNA molecule is made up of repeating units called nucleotides, each consisting of a sugar molecule, a phosphate group, and one of four nitrogenous bases — adenine, guanine, cytosine, and thymine.
The two strands (rails of the ladder) are made of alternating sugar and phosphate molecules, while the rungs of the ladder are formed by pairs of nitrogenous bases joined by hydrogen bonds — adenine always pairs with thymine, and cytosine always pairs with guanine.

practice.q: Explain Mendel's monohybrid cross using the example of plant height (tall × dwarf pea plants), stating the phenotypic ratio obtained in the F2 generation.
practice.a:
Mendel crossed pure-breeding tall pea plants (genotype TT) with pure-breeding dwarf pea plants (genotype tt) as the parental (P1) generation.
All the resulting F1 generation plants were tall (genotype Tt), showing that tallness (T) is dominant over dwarfness (t), which remained hidden/recessive.
When these F1 plants were self-fertilized, the F2 generation showed both tall and dwarf plants in a phenotypic ratio of 3 (tall) : 1 (dwarf), and a genotypic ratio of 1 (TT) : 2 (Tt) : 1 (tt).
This experiment demonstrated that hereditary factors (genes) occur in pairs, segregate during gamete formation, and combine randomly during fertilization, forming the basis of Mendel's principles of inheritance.

practice.q: Explain sickle-cell anaemia, describing its cause and symptoms.
practice.a:
Sickle-cell anaemia is a hereditary genetic disorder caused by a change in a single gene, in which the amino acid glutamic acid (normally the 6th amino acid in the haemoglobin molecule) is replaced by valine.
This alters the molecular structure of haemoglobin, causing the normally biconcave-shaped red blood cells (erythrocytes) to become sickle (crescent) shaped, especially when oxygen levels are low.
Sickle-shaped cells have a much lower oxygen-carrying capacity, tend to clump together and get destroyed easily, and can obstruct blood vessels, damaging organs like the brain, lungs, and kidneys.
Symptoms include swelling of hands and legs, joint pain, frequent colds and cough, persistent low-grade fever, exhaustion, pale face, and low haemoglobin levels.
It is inherited when both parents are carriers or sufferers, hence marriage between two carriers/sufferers of sickle-cell anaemia is discouraged.

practice.q: Explain Mendel's dihybrid cross using the cross between round-yellow seeded and wrinkled-green seeded pea plants, and state the phenotypic ratio obtained in the F2 generation.
practice.a:
Mendel crossed pure-breeding pea plants producing round, yellow seeds (genotype RRYY) with pure-breeding plants producing wrinkled, green seeds (genotype rryy), considering two pairs of contrasting characteristics simultaneously — seed shape (round R dominant over wrinkled r) and seed colour (yellow Y dominant over green y).
The parental plants produced gametes RY and ry respectively.
The resulting F1 generation plants were all round-yellow seeded (genotype RrYy), since round and yellow are both dominant traits, showing that this cross followed the same principle of dominance as seen in the monohybrid cross.
When these F1 (RrYy) plants were self-fertilized, each parent produced four types of gametes (RY, Ry, rY, ry) due to independent assortment of the two gene pairs during gamete formation.
Using a Punnett square (16 possible combinations of these gametes), the F2 generation showed four different phenotypes in the ratio 9 (round-yellow) : 3 (round-green) : 3 (wrinkled-yellow) : 1 (wrinkled-green).
This experiment led Mendel to formulate the principle of independent assortment, which states that different pairs of genes (factors) for different traits are inherited independently of one another during gamete formation, as long as they are located on different chromosomes.

practice.q: Explain the different categories of genetic disorders (chromosomal, monogenic, mitochondrial and polygenic) with one example of each.
practice.a:
Genetic disorders arise due to abnormalities in chromosome number/structure or mutations in genes, and are classified into several categories: (1) Chromosomal disorders result from numerical or structural abnormalities in chromosomes;
for example, Down syndrome is caused by trisomy of chromosome 21 (47 chromosomes instead of 46), leading to mental retardation and characteristic physical features, while Turner syndrome (44+X, monosomy of the X chromosome) and Klinefelter syndrome (44+XXY) are examples of sex chromosome abnormalities causing sterility. (2) Monogenic disorders are caused by a mutation in a single gene;
for example, sickle-cell anaemia results from a single amino acid change in the haemoglobin gene, causing red blood cells to become sickle-shaped;
