Co-ordinates of a Point: Every point has an ordered pair (x,y): the x co-ordinate (abscissa) is its perpendicular distance from the Y-axis, and the y co-ordinate (ordinate) is its perpendicular distance from the X-axis.

Equations of Axes and Parallel Lines: The X-axis is y=0 and the Y-axis is x=0. A line parallel to the X-axis is y=b (above if b>0, below if b<0); a line parallel to the Y-axis is x=a (right if a>0, left if a<0).
Formula Vault: Equation of X-axis y=0, Y-axis x=0; Line parallel to X-axis: y=b; Line parallel to Y-axis: x=a; Point P(x,y): x=distance from Y-axis, y=distance from X-axis.

flashcards.q: Who proposed the Cartesian co-ordinate system?
flashcards.a: The French mathematician René Descartes.

flashcards.q: What is the equation of the X-axis?
What is the equation of the Y-axis?
What is the equation of a line parallel to the X-axis at a distance of 4 units above it?
What is the equation of a line parallel to the Y-axis at a distance of 3 units to its left?
How are the quadrants of the plane numbered?
flashcards.a: I, II, III, IV in the anticlockwise direction, starting from the top-right.

flashcards.q: What does the x co-ordinate of a point represent?
flashcards.a: Its perpendicular distance from the Y-axis.

flashcards.q: What does the y co-ordinate of a point represent?
flashcards.a: Its perpendicular distance from the X-axis.

practice.q: Find the distance between points A(−5, 7) and B(−1, 3) using the distance formula.
Show that points A(1,2), B(4,2), C(4,6) form a right-angled triangle using the distance formula.
practice.a:
d(A,B) = √[(4−1)²+(2−2)²] = 3.
d(B,C) = √[(4−4)²+(6−2)²] = 4.
d(A,C) = √[(4−1)²+(6−2)²] = √(9+16) = 5.
Since 3² + 4² = 9+16 = 25 = 5², by the converse of Pythagoras' theorem, ΔABC is right-angled at B.

practice.q: If the distance between points P(x,4) and Q(1,4) is 5, find the possible values of x.
Derive the distance formula between two points A(x1,y1) and B(x2,y2) in a plane.
practice.a:
Let A(x1,y1) and B(x2,y2) be two points. Draw AM and BN perpendicular to the X-axis, and AC perpendicular to BN, meeting it at C.
Then AC = MN = |x2−x1| and BC = |y2−y1|.
In right triangle ACB, by Pythagoras' theorem: AB² = AC² + BC² = (x2−x1)² + (y2−y1)².
∴ AB = √[(x2−x1)² + (y2−y1)²].
This gives the distance formula d(A,B) = √[(x2−x1)² + (y2−y1)²].

practice.q: Using the distance formula, verify whether point C(2,6) is the midpoint of A(−1,2) and B(5,10), and find d(A,B).
practice.a:
d(A,C) = √[(2−(−1))²+(6−2)²] = √(9+16) = 5.
d(C,B) = √[(5−2)²+(10−6)²] = √(9+16) = 5.
d(A,B) = √[(5−(−1))²+(10−2)²] = √(36+64) = √100 = 10.
Since d(A,C) = d(C,B) = 5 and d(A,C)+d(C,B) = 10 = d(A,B), C lies on segment AB and is equidistant from A and B.
Hence C(2,6) is the midpoint of AB.

chapters.title: Trigonometry
notes: Trigonometric Ratios: Relate the angles of a right-angled triangle to the ratios of its sides: sin θ=opposite/hypotenuse, cos θ=adjacent/hypotenuse, tan θ=opposite/adjacent, and tan θ=sin θ/cos θ always.
Standard Angle Values: The values of sin, cos and tan at 0°, 30°, 45°, 60° and 90° must be memorised, since they appear constantly and cannot always be looked up in an exam.
Behaviour of sin and cos: sin θ increases from 0 to 1 as θ goes from 0° to 90°, while cos θ decreases from 1 to 0 over the same range, so sin 90°=cos 0°=1 and sin 0°=cos 90°=0.
Fundamental Identity: sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 holds for every θ from 0° to 90°, proved directly using the Pythagoras theorem in a right triangle.

Formula Vault: sin θ=opposite/hypotenuse, cos θ=adjacent/hypotenuse, tan θ=opposite/adjacent=sin θ/cos θ; sin²θ+cos²θ=1; sin 0°=0, sin 30°=1/2, sin 45°=1/√2, sin 60°=√3/2, sin 90°=1; cos 0°=1, cos 30°=√3/2, cos 45°=1/√2, cos 60°=1/2, cos 90°=0; tan 0°=0, tan 30°=1/√3, tan 45°=1, tan 60°=√3, tan 90°=not defined.

flashcards.q: Define sin θ in a right-angled triangle.
flashcards.a: Side opposite θ divided by the hypotenuse.

flashcards.q: Define cos θ in a right-angled triangle.
flashcards.a: Side adjacent to θ divided by the hypotenuse.

flashcards.q: How is tan θ related to sin θ and cos θ?
flashcards.a: tan θ = sin θ / cos θ.

flashcards.q: State the fundamental trigonometric identity.
flashcards.a: sin²θ + cos²θ = 1.

flashcards.q: What is the value of sin 30°?
What is the value of cos 60°?
What is the value of tan 45°?
What is the value of tan 90°?
flashcards.a: Not defined.

flashcards.q: What is sin 90° equal to, and why does it match cos 0°?
flashcards.a: Both equal 1, since increasing θ from 0° to 90° takes sin from 0 to 1 and cos from 1 to 0.

practice.q: If cos θ = 12/13, find the values of sin θ and tan θ.
A ladder leaning against a wall makes an angle of 60° with the ground. If the foot of the ladder is 4 m from the wall, find the length of the ladder.
practice.a:
cos 60° = base/hypotenuse = 4/ladder
⟹ ladder = 4/cos60°
= 4/(1/2)
= 8 m.

practice.q: Prove that (1 + tan²θ) = sec²θ using the definitions of trigonometric ratios in a right triangle.
practice.a:
In right triangle with angle θ, let opposite = a, adjacent = b, hypotenuse = h, so a²+b² = h² (Pythagoras).
tan θ = a/b, sec θ = h/b.
1 + tan²θ = 1 + a²/b² = (b²+a²)/b² = h²/b² = sec²θ.
Hence, 1 + tan²θ = sec²θ.

practice.q: The angle of elevation of the top of a tower from a point on the ground, 30 m away from the foot of the tower, is 30°. Find the height of the tower.
practice.a:
Let height of tower = h.
tan 30° = h/30
⟹ 1/√3 = h/30
⟹ h = 30/√3
= 10√3 m.
Hence, the height of the tower is 10√3 m (approximately 17.3 m).

practice.q: From the top of a light house 60 m high, the angles of depression of two ships on the same side are 45° and 30°. Find the distance between the ships.
practice.a:
Let the ships be at distances d1 (angle 45°) and d2 (angle 30°) from the base of the lighthouse.
tan45° = 60/d1 ⟹ d1 = 60 m.
tan30° = 60/d2 ⟹ d2 = 60√3 m.
Distance between ships = d2 − d1 = 60√3 − 60 = 60(√3−1) m ≈ 43.9 m.

chapters.title: Surface Area and Volume
notes: Cone Relations: For a cone with base radius r, height h and slant height l, l² = r² + h² (Pythagoras-based); this must be used first whenever the slant height is missing.
Cone Surface Area and Volume: Curved surface area is πrl, total surface area is πr(l+r), and volume is one-third that of a cylinder with the same base radius and height, i.e. (1/3)πr²h.
Sphere and Hemisphere: A Sphere of radius r has surface area 4πr² and volume (4/3)πr³; a Hemisphere has curved surface area 2πr², total surface area 3πr², and volume (2/3)πr³.
Cylinder: For radius r and height h, curved surface area is 2πrh, total surface area is 2πr(r+h), and volume is πr²h — always check whether curved or total surface area is asked.

Formula Vault: CONE: l²=r²+h², Curved S.A.=πrl, Total S.A.=πr(l+r), Volume=(1/3)πr²h; SPHERE: Surface Area=4πr², Volume=(4/3)πr³; HEMISPHERE: Curved S.A.=2πr², Total S.A.=3πr², Volume=(2/3)πr³; CYLINDER: Curved S.A.=2πrh, Total S.A.=2πr(r+h), Volume=πr²h; CUBE(side a): Total S.A.=6a², Volume=a³; CUBOID(l,b,h): Total S.A.=2(lb+bh+hl), Volume=l×b×h.

flashcards.q: How are the radius, height and slant height of a cone related?
flashcards.a: l² = r² + h² (Pythagoras theorem in the cone's cross-section).

flashcards.q: State the formula for the total surface area of a cone.
flashcards.a: πr(l + r), i.e. curved surface area plus the area of the base.

flashcards.q: State the formula for the volume of a cone.
flashcards.a: (1/3)πr²h — one-third of the volume of a cylinder with the same r and h.

flashcards.q: State the formula for the surface area of a sphere.
flashcards.a: 4πr².

flashcards.q: State the formula for the volume of a sphere.
State the total surface area of a hemisphere.
flashcards.a: 3πr² (curved surface 2πr² plus the flat circular base πr²).

flashcards.q: State the formula for volume of a cylinder.
flashcards.a: πr²h.

flashcards.q: State the total surface area of a cube of side a.
flashcards.a: 6a².

flashcards.q: State the volume of a cuboid with sides l, b, h.
flashcards.a: l × b × h.

practice.q: A cone has radius 6 cm and slant height 10 cm. Find its curved surface area and total surface area. (π = 3.14)
The radius of a sphere is 21 cm. Find its volume. (π = 22/7)
A cylindrical tank has diameter 14 m and height 10 m. Find the volume of water it can hold, in litres. (π = 22/7)
practice.a:
Radius = 7 m.
Volume = πr²h = (22/7) × 49 × 10 = 1540 m³.
Since 1 m³ = 1000 litres, capacity = 1540 × 1000 = 15,40,000 litres.

practice.q: A cylindrical container of radius 6 cm and height 15 cm is full of ice cream. This ice cream is to be filled into cones of radius 3 cm and height 12 cm, having a hemispherical top. Find the number of such cones needed.
practice.a:
Volume of cylinder = πr²h = π × 36 × 15 = 540π cm³.
Volume of one cone (with hemispherical top) = volume of cone + volume of hemisphere
= (1/3)π(3²)(12) + (2/3)π(3³)
= (1/3)π(9)(12) + (2/3)π(27)
= 36π + 18π
= 54π cm³.
Number of cones = 540π / 54π = 10.
Hence, 10 cones can be filled.

practice.q: A solid is in the shape of a cone mounted on a hemisphere of the same radius. If the radius is 7 cm and the height of the cone is 24 cm, find the total surface area of the solid. (π = 22/7)
practice.a:
Slant height of cone, l = √(r²+h²) = √(49+576) = √625 = 25 cm.
Curved surface area of cone = πrl = (22/7)×7×25 = 550 cm².
Curved surface area of hemisphere = 2πr² = 2×(22/7)×49 = 308 cm².
